proposals.title: Data-Driven Relay Feedback for Model-Free Adaptive Control of Robotic Manipulators
proposals.motivation: Deploying robots in unstructured environments or for novel tasks is hindered by the need for accurate, prior dynamic models, which are costly and time-consuming to derive. Existing adaptive control methods often rely on parametric models or extensive tuning. There is a critical need for controllers that can achieve high performance with minimal prior model information, enabling faster robot commissioning and adaptation to wear, payload changes, or environmental shifts.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating next-generation relay-based autotuning techniques with data-driven, memory-augmented learning architectures, a control system can autonomously learn and compensate for a robot's dynamics online, achieving robust performance without an explicit prior model.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-layer adaptive framework. The first layer employs a modified relay autotuner (inspired by Åström's recent work) to perform safe, initial excitation and establish a baseline stabilizing controller. The second layer uses a real-time capable, memory-based learning algorithm—conceptually extending the vector memory architectures used for feature extraction—to record and generalize from the state-action-response tuples generated during operation. This memory will be queried by a lightweight neural network to predict and cancel nonlinear dynamics, effectively acting as an adaptive feedforward compensator that refines itself over time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, implementable control architecture that significantly reduces the dependency on detailed robot models for adaptive control. It will contribute new algorithms for safe, data-driven controller initialization and online learning, validated on a robotic manipulator testbed. The results will advance the field of autonomous tuning and adaptive systems for robotics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific synthesis of relay autotuning (a classic Åström theme) with modern, efficient memory structures for real-time learning, creating a cohesive, model-free adaptive control pipeline. This moves beyond pure black-box learning or rigid model-based adaptation.

proposals.title: Online Recursive System Identification for Enhanced Model Reference Adaptive Control in Biomedical Actuators
proposals.motivation: Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) is crucial for precision in biomedical systems like robotic surgery or drug infusion pumps, but its performance depends on the accuracy of the initial process model. In complex, time-varying physiological environments, fixed-parameter models degrade, leading to suboptimal adaptation. Integrating real-time model learning can address this drift, improving safety and efficacy in clinical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: That integrating online, recursive system identification—specifically a Forgetting Factor Recursive Least Squares (FF-RLS) algorithm—into the MRAC framework will significantly improve reference model tracking and disturbance rejection for systems with slowly varying parameters, compared to standard MRAC with a fixed nominal model.
proposals.method: The core method is a dual-loop architecture. An inner loop employs a direct MRAC law (e.g., based on the MIT rule or Lyapunov synthesis) for fast adaptation. An outer loop uses an FF-RLS identifier to continuously estimate key plant parameters (like gain and time constant) from input-output data. These updated estimates are fed to the reference model and the adaptive law, refining the target dynamics and adaptation gains. The implementation will be tested on a simulated model of a prostate biopsy needle steering actuator, a system with nonlinear tissue interaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formalized architecture and stability analysis for merging recursive identification with MRAC, a less explored combination than identification with predictive control. It will deliver a concrete algorithm and simulation benchmarks for a biomedical use-case, demonstrating reduced steady-state error and improved transient response after parameter drift.
proposals.novelty: While system identification and adaptive control are mature fields, their tight, online integration within an MRAC structure for biomedical mechatronic systems is novel. The approach moves beyond using identification for offline tuning (autotuning) or in Model Predictive Control, applying it instead to continuously inform the reference model itself.

proposals.title: Adaptive Relay Autotuning for Processes with Large and Variable Dead Times Using a Memory-Augmented State Estimator
proposals.motivation: Processes with significant, potentially variable time delays (e.g., in chemical reactors, networked control systems, or biological processes like drug infusion) pose a major challenge for automatic controller tuning. Classical relay autotuners, foundational to Åström's work, can fail or yield poor performance when the dead time is large or changes over time, as they rely on steady-state oscillation analysis that becomes unreliable under these conditions. This limits the applicability of robust, model-free tuning in a wide range of industrial and biomedical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a learned memory mechanism to estimate and predict the delay dynamics within the autotuning framework, the modified algorithm can robustly identify process parameters and compute stable PID settings even for processes with large, unknown, or slowly varying dead times.
proposals.method: We will extend the Next-Generation Relay Autotuner architecture. The core method involves augmenting the standard relay feedback loop with a Reconfigurable Multi-Access Pattern Vector Memory (as used in ORB feature extraction) to act as a temporal buffer and state estimator for the process output. This memory module will store recent I/O data patterns, enabling the algorithm to correlate non-concurrent signals and estimate the current effective dead time using a lightweight, online optimization routine. The estimated delay will then adapt the relay experiment's evaluation phase and directly inform the subsequent tuning rules, which will be derived from modified describing function analysis for dead-time systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, model-free autotuning tool for a challenging class of processes, expanding the domain of applicability of relay methods. It will contribute a novel synthesis of control theory and embedded memory architectures, demonstrating how algorithmic memory can explicitly manage temporal constraints in real-time tuning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of a reconfigurable vector memory unit into the autotuner's feedback path to actively estimate and compensate for dead time, moving beyond post-experiment analysis to an adaptive, state-aware tuning procedure. This approach is distinct from prior Smith predictor or model-based methods as it remains within the model-free relay paradigm.

proposals.title: Adaptive Relay-Based Autotuning for Nonlinear and Time-Varying Systems via Incremental Model Learning
proposals.motivation: Classical relay autotuning, a cornerstone of Åström's work, assumes linear time-invariant plant dynamics, limiting its application to complex real-world systems like biomedical processes (e.g., drug delivery, physiological control) or industrial loops with wear and drift. Extending this robust, experiment-driven framework to handle mild nonlinearities and parameter drift is a critical open problem in adaptive control, enabling wider use of automatic tuning in safety-critical and variable environments.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a short-term memory of relay experiment data and employing incremental kernel-based or locally linear model learning, the core relay feedback experiment can be periodically adapted to identify and compensate for process nonlinearities and slow parameter variations, maintaining robust performance.
proposals.method: The method extends the Next Generation Relay Autotuner architecture. A reconfigurable vector memory (inspired by recent ORB feature extraction work) will store time-stamped sequences of input-output relay oscillations. An incremental learning algorithm, such as a Recursive Kernel Ridge Regression or a Just-In-Time model, will analyze this data to detect drift and estimate a local linear model or a Hammerstein-type nonlinearity. This model will then inform an adaptive rule to adjust the relay amplitude or a subsequent tuning step, creating an iterative 'autotune-and-adapt' loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel autotuning algorithm that bridges the gap between simple relay methods and complex nonlinear adaptive control, offering a practitioner-friendly tool for systems with slow variations. It will deliver a formal analysis of stability and convergence for the proposed incremental learning within a relay framework and demonstrate efficacy on benchmarks including a simulated model of arterial pressure dynamics with time-varying compliance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of Åström's relay autotuning philosophy with modern incremental machine learning for model adaptation, creating a memory-aware tuner that respects the simplicity and experiment-driven nature of the original method while explicitly addressing its core limitation regarding time-variance and nonlinearity.

proposals.title: Experimental Validation of a Next-Generation Relay Autotuner on a Multi-Process Thermal Control Loop
proposals.motivation: Relay autotuning, pioneered by Åström and Hägglund, is a cornerstone of industrial PID control. While recent algorithmic advancements, such as the 'Next Generation Relay Autotuners' that improve robustness and convergence, have been proposed and analyzed in simulation, their practical performance on real hardware with non-ideal dynamics, noise, and actuator constraints remains unverified. Physical validation is a critical step to bridge the gap between theoretical algorithm development and deployable industrial tools.
proposals.hypothesis: The proposed next-generation relay autotuning algorithm will demonstrate superior robustness and consistent convergence to accurate PID parameters on a physical thermal control testbed, outperforming classical relay feedback in the presence of significant sensor noise and actuator saturation.
proposals.method: We will implement the proposed autotuning algorithm on a real-time platform (e.g., a microcontroller or a compactRIO) interfaced with a multi-zone thermal process control rig. The experimental protocol will involve conducting a series of tuning experiments under varying conditions of noise injection, setpoint changes, and deliberate actuator saturation limits. Performance will be quantitatively compared against a classical relay autotuner using metrics such as tuning time, overshoot in the subsequent closed-loop step response, and the IAE (Integral of Absolute Error) of the controlled system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental evidence for the robustness claims of the next-generation autotuner, translating a theoretical algorithm into a validated tool. It will establish practical guidelines for its implementation and identify any unforeseen hardware-level challenges, directly contributing to the maturation of the method for industrial adoption.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in the algorithm itself, but in its rigorous experimental validation on a physical system, explicitly testing its performance against the key practical disturbances (noise, saturation) that simulation-based analyses often idealize.

proposals.title: Microsecond Autotuning: Implementation and Analysis of Relay-Based Autotuners on Resource-Constrained Microcontrollers
proposals.motivation: Autotuning, a cornerstone of Åström's legacy, enables adaptive control for systems with unknown or varying dynamics. While recent work has advanced relay-based autotuners for industrial processes, their deployment in low-cost, small-scale embedded applications (e.g., micro-drones, portable medical devices, IoT actuators) is hindered by assumptions of substantial computational resources and millisecond-scale sampling. Enabling microsecond-scale autotuning on low-power microcontrollers would unlock adaptive control for a new class of fast, miniaturized systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That a streamlined, memory-optimized implementation of a next-generation relay autotuner can execute a complete tuning cycle within microsecond timeframes on a Cortex-M class microcontroller without sacrificing the algorithm's convergence and robustness properties.
proposals.method: We will adapt the 'Next Generation Relay Autotuner' algorithm for ARM Cortex-M4/M7 cores. The implementation will leverage the researcher's recent work on reconfigurable vector memory architectures to manage the time-series data of the relay experiment efficiently. Key steps include: 1) Fixed-point arithmetic optimization of the limit-cycle analysis, 2) Design of a deterministic, interrupt-driven task scheduler for microsecond-precise timing, and 3) Integration of a minimal Gleason-score-inspired heuristic to classify system response and adjust tuning aggressiveness, akin to methods used in medical diagnostic systems. Validation will use a programmable electronic load to emulate first- and second-order systems with variable parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first publicly available, benchmarked implementation of an industrial-grade autotuner for microsecond embedded systems. It will establish practical limits for sampling rates and tuning accuracy on low-power cores and produce a generalized software framework for embedded adaptive control. This directly extends Åström's autotuning principles to the domain of ultra-fast, resource-constrained devices.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the extreme temporal scaling of the autotuning algorithm and the co-design of the control algorithm with a dedicated, real-time memory access pattern, moving beyond theoretical analysis to a hardware-aware implementation validated at microsecond timescales.

proposals.title: A Relay-Based Autotuner with Oscillation Mode Analysis for Improved PID Tuning in Systems with Dominant Resonant Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Classical relay feedback autotuning, pioneered by Åström and Hägglund, is a cornerstone of industrial PID tuning but can yield poor performance for systems with significant oscillatory modes, such as mechanical resonances or underdamped processes. Existing methods often misinterpret the dominant oscillation, leading to aggressive or unstable controller parameters. This problem is pertinent in areas like precision motion control and process industries where such dynamics are common.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a real-time oscillation mode analysis algorithm into the relay experiment, we can accurately distinguish the process's fundamental oscillatory mode from higher harmonics or noise, enabling the derivation of more robust PID parameters that explicitly account for the system's resonant characteristics.
proposals.method: We will extend the Next Generation Relay Autotuner framework. The core innovation is a multi-access pattern vector memory system (inspired by recent real-time feature extraction work) to store and time-stamp relay oscillation cycles. A dedicated signal processing module, employing algorithms like Prony's method or a tailored deep neural network (informed by prior work on temporal pattern detection from medical signals), will analyze the stored waveform to identify the primary oscillation frequency and damping ratio. These identified parameters will then feed a modified version of the Ziegler-Nichols or AMIGO tuning rules, incorporating a stability margin constraint for oscillatory plants.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, industrially-relevant autotuning method with demonstrably superior performance for a challenging class of systems. It contributes a formalized procedure for oscillation mode identification within the autotuning context and a set of validated tuning correlations for underdamped and resonant processes.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of real-time spectral feature extraction hardware/software with the classical relay experiment to explicitly target and characterize oscillatory dynamics for control design, moving beyond the standard ultimate gain and period assumption.

proposals.title: Continuous Auto-Tuning via Recursive System Identification for Adaptive Control of Biomedical Systems
proposals.motivation: Auto-tuners, a core legacy of Åström's work, are essential for commissioning PID controllers but are typically one-shot procedures. In biomedical applications like drug infusion or physiological control, patient dynamics (e.g., response to anesthesia or vasoactive drugs) can change significantly over time due to factors like disease progression or circadian rhythms. A static model from initial tuning becomes suboptimal, risking degraded performance or safety.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating online, recursive system identification algorithms into an auto-tuning framework can continuously update a process model, enabling adaptive controller re-tuning that maintains optimal performance despite slow or abrupt changes in system dynamics.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-mode architecture combining a relay-based autotuner (for safe initial excitation/identification) with a continuously active Recursive Least Squares (RLS) algorithm with variable forgetting factor. The RLS will operate on closed-loop data, using a low-level dither signal or natural setpoint variations for persistent excitation. Upon significant model parameter drift, exceeding a statistical confidence bound, the autotuner's core algorithm will be triggered to compute and implement new PID parameters. The implementation will be tested on a high-fidelity simulator of prostate cancer radiotherapy, where patient motion and tissue response vary, and on a cardiovascular model for blood pressure control.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the concept of autotuning from a commissioning tool to a lifelong adaptive control component. It will provide a formal methodology for safe, continuous adaptation in slowly varying systems, bridging the gap between one-shot autotuners and full adaptive control. The results will be directly applicable to biomedical control systems requiring long-term, robust autonomy.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive control and autotuning are mature fields, the novel integration lies in using the autotuner's robust identification logic as a supervisory layer to validate and act upon continuous RLS estimates, creating a cautious, event-driven adaptation scheme specifically designed for safety-critical applications where aggressive continuous adaptation is undesirable.

proposals.title: Adaptive Control with Online Model Order Selection via Recursive Estimation and Memory-Augmented Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: Classical adaptive control, including the autotuning methods pioneered by Åström, often requires a priori knowledge of the system order, a restrictive assumption for complex, evolving systems like biological processes or reconfigurable hardware. This limitation hinders the application of robust control to modern challenges in medical devices (e.g., cardiovascular dynamics) and embedded systems where dimensionality may be uncertain or time-varying. Relaxing this assumption is a fundamental step toward more autonomous and resilient controllers.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive control framework that integrates online, recursive model order selection with parameter estimation can maintain stability and performance for linear time-varying systems with unknown or drifting dimensionality. This can be achieved by combining Bayesian model evidence with a memory-augmented neural network to manage and recall effective model structures.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-time-scale algorithm. On a slower scale, a recursive Bayesian model comparison, akin to a Gleason-score-like evidence accumulator, will continuously evaluate the likelihood of candidate model orders based on prediction error. On a faster scale, a Reconfigurable Multi-Access Pattern Vector Memory will store and provide fast access to parameter sets and state estimates for the currently active model order. A deep neural network, pre-trained on spectral features from system identification data (inspired by scintigraphy analysis), will serve as a prior for initializing plausible model structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a theoretically grounded and practically implementable adaptive control algorithm that does not require fixed, known system order. It will extend the autotuning paradigm to a broader class of 'black-box' systems. The integration of a dedicated vector memory architecture ensures the method's feasibility for real-time applications in embedded and medical systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of recursive model order selection within the control loop, moving beyond parameter estimation for a fixed structure. The use of a hardware-inspired reconfigurable memory to manage multiple concurrent models in real-time is a distinct systems engineering contribution not found in purely statistical model selection literature.

proposals.title: Concurrent Learning and Robust Monitoring for Modular Adaptive Control with Unknown Actuation Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Adaptive control systems often fail when the control direction (sign of the high-frequency gain) and magnitude are completely unknown, a non-trivial problem in safety-critical applications like robotics and medical devices. Existing solutions rely on restrictive assumptions like persistent excitation or Nussbaum gains, which can lead to poor transient performance or parameter drift. This work addresses the need for a stable, high-performance controller that can operate without prior actuation knowledge, a key challenge in deploying adaptive systems in uncertain environments.
proposals.hypothesis: A modular adaptive controller integrating a concurrent learning estimator with a robust monitoring function and an event-triggered switching mechanism can guarantee global stability and achieve rapid convergence to high-performance model-based adaptation for systems with fully unknown actuation dynamics, without requiring persistent excitation.
proposals.method: The core is a modular architecture separating the tasks of exploration and adaptation. An event-triggered switching mechanism, governed by a monitoring function, will efficiently explore the control direction to prevent instability. A concurrent learning estimator will populate and analyze a history stack of input-output data to simultaneously identify the actuation sign and gain magnitude. This learned model will then enable a rapid, stable switch to a high-performance model-reference adaptive control (MRAC) law, with the monitoring function providing robustness against parameter drift.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a theoretically-grounded, practical framework for adaptive control under extreme actuation uncertainty, eliminating the need for persistent excitation. It will deliver a novel integration of concurrent learning for fast parameter identification with robust monitoring for guaranteed stability, advancing the field of safe learning-based control.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious synthesis lies in using concurrent learning—a data-driven method from machine learning—specifically to resolve the fundamental control direction problem in real-time, coupled with a formal switching logic to guarantee stability throughout the exploration and adaptation phases.

proposals.title: Nussbaum-Gain-Inspired Adaptive Control with Memory-Augmented Parameter Estimation for Systems with Unknown Control Direction
proposals.motivation: In adaptive control, the assumption of known control direction (sign of the high-frequency gain) is often critical for stability but is unrealistic in many complex systems, such as biomedical devices with uncertain patient dynamics or robotics with changing actuator configurations. Existing Nussbaum-gain methods can handle this but often exhibit poor transient performance and lack systematic integration with modern data-driven estimation techniques. This gap limits the robustness and applicability of adaptive schemes in safety-critical domains.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a Nussbaum-gain core with a memory-augmented, data-driven parameter estimator, an adaptive control scheme can achieve global stability and satisfactory transient performance for linear and certain nonlinear systems without any prior knowledge of the control direction.
proposals.method: The core method will employ a classical Nussbaum gain (e.g., N(κ)=κ²cos(κ)) in the control law to explore the unknown direction. Crucially, this will be coupled with a novel parameter estimator inspired by the 'Reconfigurable Multi-Access Pattern Vector Memory' concept from recent computer vision work. This estimator will maintain a time-windowed memory of past regressor vectors and estimation errors, enabling a form of retrospective cost optimization to improve the convergence of the parameter estimates, akin to the data re-use seen in certain relay autotuning methods. Stability will be analyzed using Lyapunov and averaging theory for switched systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, practical adaptive control algorithm with rigorous stability proofs for the unknown direction problem, explicitly bridging classic control theory and modern memory-based learning. It will deliver a tool for control engineers facing deep parametric uncertainty, with potential applications in medical devices and autonomous systems where model parameters can change sign.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the structured fusion of the classical Nussbaum-gain technique with a reconfigurable memory-based estimation architecture, creating a hybrid algorithm that uses historical data intelligently to mitigate the well-known performance limitations of pure Nussbaum-gain approaches.

proposals.title: Provable Runtime Safety for Systems with Unobservable Modes via Adaptive Set-Membership Filtering and Control Barrier Functions
proposals.motivation: Modern control systems, from medical devices to autonomous agents, increasingly operate with limited sensing, leading to partially observable and uncertain dynamics. Pre-2026 safety-critical methods like Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) often assume full state knowledge or simplistic uncertainty models, which is unrealistic and can lead to unsafe behavior if unobservable or poorly modeled modes become excited. A framework that provides rigorous, real-time safety certificates under these conditions is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating an adaptive set-membership state estimator, which produces a high-probability bound on the full state error inclusive of unobservable directions, with a runtime safety filter based on robust CBFs, we can guarantee system safety with quantifiable confidence despite significant model uncertainty and limited outputs.
proposals.method: We will develop an adaptive set-membership filter that extends interval observer concepts to explicitly quantify uncertainty growth in unobservable subspaces, using online parameter adaptation akin to relay autotuning for bounding uncertainty. This real-time, ellipsoidal or interval state bound will be passed to a Safety Filter module. The filter will use robust CBF constraints, formulated as a quadratic program, to minimally modify any primary control signal (e.g., from a performance-driven AI controller) to ensure the entire state uncertainty set remains within the safe set with a specified probability.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel adaptive estimation framework that provides rigorous, real-time bounds on full-state uncertainty for partially observable systems. 2) A practically implementable runtime assurance architecture that delivers verifiable safety guarantees for use with any legacy or learning-based controller, maximizing performance while ensuring safety.
proposals.novelty: The explicit, adaptive quantification of uncertainty in the unobservable subspace for real-time safety certification, moving beyond observability assumptions or static uncertainty models, and its direct integration into a minimally invasive safety filter.

proposals.title: Sparse-Dynamics Observer: Performance-Guaranteed State Estimation via Compressed Sensing and l1-Regularization
proposals.motivation: Advanced control and diagnostic systems, such as those in medical devices or process automation, often rely on accurate state estimation from limited, noisy sensor outputs. Traditional observer designs require a sufficient number of measurements to guarantee performance, which is costly or infeasible in applications like implantable monitors or distributed sensor networks. The success of compressed sensing in reconstructing high-quality medical images from severely undersampled data (e.g., in MRI) suggests a powerful analogy for dynamical systems where state trajectories are often sparse or compressible in a suitable basis.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating state reconstruction as a compressed sensing problem that jointly enforces sparsity in a transform domain and consistency with a known (possibly uncertain) dynamical model, it is possible to design an observer that provides rigorous performance guarantees on estimation error using only partial, noisy output measurements.
proposals.method: We will formulate a receding-horizon estimation problem as a convex optimization, minimizing a cost function with an l1-regularization term on the state's representation in a Fourier or wavelet basis, subject to dynamics and measurement constraints. The core algorithm will be a real-time solver based on the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM), tailored for embedded implementation. Theoretical analysis will derive bounds on the estimation error by studying the Restricted Isometry Property (RIP)-like conditions of the system's extended observability matrix in the chosen sparse basis, linking compressibility to guaranteed performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel observer design framework with explicit performance bounds for systems with compressible state trajectories, reducing the required sensor infrastructure. It will deliver a practical, real-time capable algorithm validated in a case study, such as estimating cardiovascular states from sparse physiological signals, bridging concepts from diagnostic imaging and control theory.
proposals.novelty: The approach is non-obvious as it directly transplants the core philosophy of medical compressed sensing—exploiting sparsity for reconstruction from few measurements—into the domain of dynamical state estimation, creating a hybrid between sparse optimization and model-based observer theory to relax traditional observability requirements.

proposals.title: Performance-Guaranteed Output-Feedback Control via Learned Memory-Augmented Observers
proposals.motivation: Advanced control algorithms often rely on full state measurement, which is infeasible in many real-world systems like biomedical devices or robotics, where only limited, noisy outputs are available. Pre-2026, the integration of data-driven learning with robust control theory to achieve formal guarantees under output feedback remains a significant challenge. This work directly addresses the gap between high-performance model-based control and practical sensor limitations.
proposals.hypothesis: By augmenting a robust output-feedback controller with a learned, reconfigurable memory module that acts as a nonlinear observer, we can recover the performance guarantees of full-state feedback using only noisy partial measurements. The memory structure can be trained to encode the relevant latent system dynamics.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-loop architecture: an inner loop with a robust output-feedback controller (e.g., based on Åström's relay autotuning principles for stability) and an outer, data-driven memory module inspired by the reconfigurable vector memory used for ORB features. This module will be implemented as a recurrent neural network or a differentiable memory bank, trained via deep learning (akin to methods in scintigraphy analysis) on historical input-output data to predict the missing state information. The combined system's stability and performance will be analyzed using dissipativity theory or small-gain arguments.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel framework for co-designing learning-based observers with robust controllers, delivering formal performance certificates. It will yield a practical method applicable to systems like medical imaging devices or process control, where sensor access is limited. The work bridges Åström's legacy in adaptive control with modern machine learning for real-time systems.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious synthesis lies in using a reconfigurable, trainable memory as an integral part of the control loop's state estimation, explicitly designed to preserve stability margins, rather than treating the observer and controller as separate, cascaded components. This moves beyond standard Kalman filtering or linear observer design.

proposals.title: Compressed Sensing for State Reconstruction: An l1-Optimization Approach to Output-Feedback Control of Complex Nonlinear Systems
proposals.motivation: Output-feedback control for complex nonlinear systems remains a fundamental challenge, as standard observer designs often fail for systems with high-dimensional, poorly measured, or sparsely interconnected states. Pre-2026, compressed sensing revolutionized signal processing by enabling the reconstruction of sparse signals from limited measurements via convex optimization. This research proposes to bridge these fields, addressing the control problem where the system's internal state acts as a structured 'signal' to be reconstructed from sparse output data for real-time stabilization.
proposals.hypothesis: The unmeasured state of a broad class of complex nonlinear systems can be actively and reliably reconstructed in real-time by formulating a receding-horizon l1-norm optimization problem that enforces known structural constraints (e.g., bounded Jacobians, sector nonlinearities, or sparse interconnection graphs), thereby enabling a systematic output-feedback law for stabilization where traditional observers are infeasible.
proposals.method: The approach will formalize the state reconstruction as a constrained optimization problem solved online. A receding-horizon estimator will minimize a cost function combining an l1-norm penalty on the state prediction error (promoting sparsity in the innovation) and a term encoding system dynamics, subject to convex constraints derived from sector bounds or Lipschitz conditions on the nonlinearities. This will be integrated with a model predictive control (MPC) law, using the reconstructed state as the initial condition. Implementation will leverage efficient convex solvers (e.g., based on ADMM) and be tested on benchmark problems, including networked systems with sparse interconnections, reflecting the researcher's recent work on reconfigurable memory architectures for real-time feature extraction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for output-feedback control of nonlinear systems that are not amenable to standard observer design, extending the applicability of rigorous control methods. It will deliver a concrete algorithm, with stability and performance guarantees, that merges tools from compressed sensing and nonlinear control. The results will be validated through simulation studies on complex system models, potentially including biomedical dynamics relevant to the researcher's recent diagnostic network work.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious core is treating the state reconstruction problem explicitly as a compressed sensing task under nonlinear dynamic constraints, moving beyond traditional Luenberger or Kalman filter paradigms. This allows the direct exploitation of structural sparsity and nonlinearity bounds within a convex optimization loop for feedback control, a synthesis not previously systematized.

proposals.title: Concurrent Neural Identification and Adaptive Backstepping Control for Unstable Nonlinear Plants
proposals.motivation: Controlling unstable nonlinear systems with unknown dynamics remains a significant challenge in robotics and process control. Traditional methods often separate identification and stabilization, which is inefficient and risky for unstable plants. A unified approach that safely learns the model while stabilizing the system in real-time is needed to improve autonomy and performance in safety-critical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural network embedded within an adaptive backstepping control framework can simultaneously stabilize an unstable nonlinear system and serve as a parametric model for online concurrent identification, leading to co-improving control performance and model accuracy.
proposals.method: We will develop an algorithm where Radial Basis Function (RBF) neural networks approximate unknown system nonlinearities within a recursive backstepping procedure, ensuring boundedness via Lyapunov-based adaptive laws. The same network weights will be updated by a concurrent gradient-based identifier, using the closed-loop state and control signals as a persistently exciting training set. Stability will be proven using a composite Lyapunov function, and the method will be validated on a simulated unstable bioreactor or inverted pendulum system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a theoretically-grounded framework for safe, online learning control of unstable systems. It will contribute a new algorithm that unifies identification and stabilization, reducing the need for separate experimental modeling phases and enabling adaptive performance improvement during operation.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the dual-purpose use of the neural network as both the stabilizing function approximator and the concurrent identification model within a single, stable adaptive control loop, a synthesis not fully explored in pre-2026 adaptive control literature.

proposals.title: Adaptive Neural Backstepping Control with Lyapunov-Stable Function Approximation for Uncertain Nonlinear Systems
proposals.motivation: Backstepping is a powerful recursive design technique for nonlinear control, but its application is limited by the need for precise knowledge of system dynamics. Real-world systems, such as biomedical actuators or robotic manipulators, contain unknown nonlinearities and time-varying parameters. Pre-2026, integrating universal approximators like neural networks into the backstepping framework to handle these uncertainties remains a challenging and active area, crucial for robust autonomy in complex physical systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That a recursive control law, integrating Lyapunov-based adaptive updates for a deep neural network's weights, can guarantee uniform ultimate boundedness (UUB) of the closed-loop system while approximating unknown nonlinear functions during the backstepping procedure.
proposals.method: We will develop a recursive design for strict-feedback systems where a feedforward neural network (FNN) with a single hidden layer and tanh activation approximates the lumped unknown dynamics at each step. The key technical approach is to derive tuning laws for the FNN output layer weights directly from a candidate Lyapunov function, ensuring stability. The method will be implemented and tested in simulation on benchmark systems (e.g., inverted pendulum, wing rock dynamics) and a real-time relay autotuner setup, leveraging expertise in implementation from recent relay autotuner work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, stable architecture for neural-adaptive backstepping, contributing a new design template with provable UUB stability. It will bridge Åström's legacy in adaptive control and recent group work on real-time implementation and pattern extraction, demonstrating practical viability beyond theoretical analysis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific synthesis of Lyapunov-derived weight adaptation within the recursive backstepping structure, moving beyond standard gradient descent, and in the planned validation on real-time control hardware, connecting deep learning function approximation to proven autotuning implementation frameworks.

proposals.title: Safe and Informative Dual-Optimal Control: A Barrier-Lyapunov Framework for Real-Time Parameter Learning
proposals.motivation: Automatic controller tuning and system identification often require informative experiments, which can push a system towards instability, especially in safety-critical applications like medical devices or process control. Existing methods, such as relay feedback or cautious excitation, either compromise safety for information or are too conservative, leading to slow learning. A framework that rigorously guarantees stability during active probing is needed to enable fast, safe adaptation in uncertain systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating a real-time, constrained optimization problem that integrates stability certificates (e.g., Control Barrier Functions) with an information-theoretic objective (e.g., Fisher Information), one can synthesize control actions that simultaneously ensure safe operation and maximize the rate of parameter identification.
proposals.method: We will develop a receding-horizon optimal control algorithm. The cost function will maximize the trace or determinant of the Fisher Information Matrix for a linear parameter-varying model. Hard constraints will be imposed via a Control Barrier Function (CBF) derived from a known Lyapunov function to guarantee input-to-state stability. The optimization will be solved in real-time using a tailored solver, potentially leveraging the researcher's experience with reconfigurable memory architectures for efficient matrix computations in embedded systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel control design methodology that unifies safe exploration and parameter learning, yielding a provably stable adaptive controller. It will contribute a practical algorithm for safe autotuning in applications where operational constraints are paramount, extending the principles of relay autotuners and cautious excitation to a more rigorous, optimal framework.
proposals.novelty: The tight, online co-design of information-maximizing excitation and stability-preserving control through constrained optimization is a significant departure from sequential or switched approaches. Integrating CBFs directly into the information objective for real-time safe learning is a novel synthesis of tools from adaptive control and safety-critical systems.

proposals.title: Controller-Guided Optimal Probing for Parameter Estimation in Dynamical Systems: An Analogy from Seismic Full-Waveform Inversion
proposals.motivation: Precise parameter estimation is critical for robust control and model-based diagnostics in complex systems, such as biomedical or mechatronic systems. Traditional identification methods often use generic excitation signals, which may be suboptimal for specific uncertain parameters and can compromise stability or performance during the experiment. There is a need for a framework that actively designs safe, informative experiments by leveraging the stabilizing controller itself.
proposals.hypothesis: A stabilizing feedback controller can be co-designed to generate optimal probing inputs that maximize the Fisher information for a selected set of uncertain system parameters, thereby transforming the regulator into an active experiment design tool without sacrificing closed-loop stability.
proposals.method: We will formulate an optimal control problem where the cost function combines a Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) metric for target parameters with standard regulation penalties. The solution, analogous to optimal seismic source design in full-waveform inversion, will yield a dual-purpose control law. Implementation will leverage iterative gradient-based algorithms (like the ones used in relay autotuning) to compute the probing signals. The 'Reconfigurable Multi-Access Pattern Vector Memory' architecture will be adapted to manage the real-time data streams of probing inputs and system responses efficiently.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new methodology for parameter-adaptive system identification where the experiment is intelligently shaped by the control system. It will yield a theoretical framework and practical algorithms for dual-use controllers that stabilize and probe, enhancing the precision of models used in critical applications like medical device control or fault detection.
proposals.novelty: While optimal experiment design exists in system identification, the novel integration of the stabilizing controller as the active probing agent—directly inspired by geophysical exploration techniques—and the focus on maximizing information for specific, uncertain parameters within a closed-loop operation is a significant departure from standard open-loop persistent excitation methods.

proposals.title: Co-Design of Persistent Excitation and Nussbaum-Gain Control for Black-Box System Identification with Unknown Direction
proposals.motivation: Classical adaptive control and system identification require prior knowledge of the control direction (the sign of the high-frequency gain), which is often unavailable in complex, poorly modeled systems like biomedical processes or relay-based autotuners. This limitation hinders the creation of reliable digital twins and autonomous tuning in safety-critical applications. A unified strategy that simultaneously learns the direction and identifies parameters from a 'cold start' is a fundamental open problem in adaptive systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a reconfigurable, multi-pattern excitation signal with a Nussbaum-gain-based adaptive controller, it is possible to guarantee global stability and parameter convergence for linear time-invariant systems without any prior structural knowledge, including the control direction.
proposals.method: The core technical approach merges tools from Åström's relay autotuning and adaptive control legacy. A Nussbaum-type gain function will be embedded within a gradient-based parameter update law to handle the unknown direction. The excitation signal will be generated by a reconfigurable vector memory unit, inspired by recent work on ORB feature extraction, to store and schedule orthogonal probing sequences (e.g., modified PRBS). A supervisory algorithm will modulate the amplitude and frequency content of this signal based on real-time estimates of the covariance matrix to ensure persistent excitation without compromising transient stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a provably stable, unified algorithm for full black-box identification and stabilization, removing a key theoretical and practical barrier in adaptive control. It will deliver a concrete framework for autonomous digital twin initialization in uncertain industrial and biomedical systems, directly applicable to problems like model-based cancer therapy control or cardiovascular system monitoring.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious fusion lies in the explicit co-design of the probing signal's memory pattern with the Nussbaum-gain stability mechanism, creating a single adaptive law that concurrently solves the direction learning and parameter estimation problems, whereas prior methods typically address them separately or require restarting.

proposals.title: Optimal Control via Backstepping: A Lyapunov-Function-Based Framework for Performance Optimization
proposals.motivation: The backstepping methodology is a cornerstone of nonlinear control design, primarily used to guarantee stability. However, in modern applications like robotics and process control, stability alone is insufficient; optimal performance with respect to energy, time, or resource use is critical. Existing optimal control methods (e.g., Dynamic Programming, MPC) can be computationally intensive, while backstepping offers a structured, recursive design. Bridging this gap by embedding performance metrics directly into the backstepping procedure is a significant open problem with broad practical implications.
proposals.hypothesis: By reformulating the backstepping virtual control laws as the solution to a sequence of embedded, low-dimensional optimal control problems, one can systematically derive stabilizing controllers that are also optimal with respect to a meaningful, user-defined cost functional.
proposals.method: The core method involves a novel recursive algorithm where, at each step of the backstepping procedure, the virtual control is chosen not only to stabilize the corresponding subsystem but also to minimize a local cost integral. This transforms the traditional Lyapunov derivative condition into a Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB)-type inequality. We will employ function approximation (e.g., neural networks or polynomial approximators, inspired by recent work on Gleason scoring and feature extraction) to solve the local HJB equations efficiently. The final control law will be implemented and tested on a benchmark nonlinear system, such as a flexible joint robot or a chemical reactor model, comparing its performance (settling time, control effort) against standard backstepping and LQR designs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new design framework that unifies Lyapunov-based stability and optimality within the backstepping architecture. It will provide control engineers with a systematic, computationally tractable tool for designing performance-optimized nonlinear controllers. The resulting algorithm will be a significant addition to the toolkit for real-time control systems, extending Åström's legacy in autotuning and practical implementation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the dual formulation of the backstepping step as a local optimal control problem, moving beyond pure stabilization. While adaptive and robust backstepping exist, the explicit, stepwise minimization of a global performance cost through this recursive structure is a non-obvious and unexplored synthesis of optimal control theory and Lyapunov design.

proposals.title: Stability and Performance Analysis of Sampled-Data Systems with Aperiodic Sampling: A Hybrid Systems and Data-Driven Approach
proposals.motivation: Modern cyber-physical and networked control systems increasingly rely on event-triggered or multi-rate sensing, leading to inherently non-uniform sampling. Classical sampled-data theory, built on the assumption of a constant period, provides overly conservative or inaccurate stability and performance guarantees for these systems. A rigorous analytical framework for aperiodic sampling is crucial for designing reliable systems in robotics, process control, and medical devices where sensor data arrives irregularly.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling aperiodically sampled systems as switched linear systems with constrained switching, and by leveraging data from system identification, it is possible to derive less conservative stability conditions and performance bounds than those provided by classical robust or periodic approaches.
proposals.method: The approach will combine hybrid systems theory and data-driven methods. First, the system will be modeled as a linear switched system where each switch corresponds to a sampling instance. We will employ a piecewise quadratic Lyapunov function framework, with constraints derived from the known minimum and maximum sampling intervals (range known or identified). Second, inspired by recent autotuning and relay-based identification techniques (Gleason/Aström legacy), we will develop an identification procedure to characterize the distribution of sampling intervals from experimental or simulation data, informing the switching constraints. Stability analysis will use Linear Matrix Inequalities (LMIs) to compute a maximum admissible sampling jitter.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, less conservative analytical toolset for certifying stability and quantifying performance degradation in systems with aperiodic sampling. It will bridge the gap between classical identification/autotuning practices and modern hybrid systems analysis, offering a methodology that is both theoretically grounded and practically informed by system data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of data-driven identification of sampling patterns (inspired by relay autotuners) into the formal stability analysis of hybrid system models, moving beyond treating jitter as merely an unknown bounded disturbance.

proposals.title: Resource-Constrained Autotuning and Anomaly Detection for High-Order Sampled Systems: A Memory-Aware Algorithmic Framework
proposals.motivation: Modern control and diagnostic systems, from medical imaging to industrial processes, increasingly rely on high-order sampled models and deep neural networks, which are computationally intensive. The assumption of unlimited processing power and memory is untenable for real-time, embedded, or large-scale deployments, leading to performance degradation or system failure under resource constraints. This gap is critical in safety-sensitive applications like the real-time analysis of cardiac perfusion data or autonomous system tuning.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling computational and memory constraints within the control and analysis loop, we can develop novel, resource-aware algorithms that maintain stability and diagnostic fidelity for high-order systems, where traditional methods would fail or require excessive resources.
proposals.method: We will extend Åström's relay autotuner framework by integrating a reconfigurable vector memory architecture (inspired by recent ORB feature extraction work) to manage state and gradient data efficiently. The core will be a constrained optimization algorithm that dynamically adjusts model order and sampling rates based on available computational budget, using techniques from model predictive control and pruning. For anomaly detection (e.g., in coronary artery disease screening from scintigraphy), we will develop a cascaded neural network where a lightweight 'gatekeeper' model filters data, invoking high-order diagnostic models only when necessary.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal methodology for co-designing control/analysis algorithms with their computational platforms, yielding practical stability and performance guarantees under resource limits. It will deliver specific algorithmic templates for resource-constrained autotuning and medical diagnostic systems, directly applicable to embedded and edge computing scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond optimizing algorithms in isolation to jointly optimizing the algorithm and its memory/compute footprint in real-time, a synthesis not deeply explored in classical control theory or mainstream machine learning before 2026. It uniquely bridges Åström's control legacy with contemporary constraints of embedded AI.

proposals.title: Efficient Computation of OPENAT Transfer Functions for Sampled-Data Systems Using Structured Matrix Methods and Reconfigurable Memory Architectures
proposals.motivation: The OPENAT (Open-loop Pulse Transfer Function) is fundamental for analyzing stability and performance in sampled-data control systems, such as those in modern relay autotuners and medical devices. Pre-2026, its computation for high-order or multi-rate systems can be numerically intensive and memory-bound, limiting real-time application in complex systems like those for diagnostic imaging or adaptive control. Improving these algorithms addresses a core bottleneck in the design and analysis of cyber-physical systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging structured matrix factorizations (e.g., Toeplitz, Schur) and a reconfigurable multi-access pattern memory architecture, the computational complexity and memory footprint of OPENAT transfer function calculation can be significantly reduced while maintaining numerical robustness.
proposals.method: We will formalize the OPENAT computation as a structured linear algebra problem, exploiting the near-Toeplitz structure of the associated matrices derived from zero-order-hold discretization. Algorithms will be developed using pre-2026 numerical linear algebra libraries (e.g., LAPACK) optimized for these structures. Inspired by Åström's recent work on vector memories for ORB features, we will design a simulated reconfigurable memory controller to optimize data access patterns (row, column, diagonal) for the matrix operations central to the computation, reducing access latency and improving cache efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver new, publicly available algorithms for faster and more memory-efficient sampled-data analysis, directly benefiting control system design tools. It will also demonstrate a novel cross-domain application of reconfigurable memory architectures from computer vision to computational control theory, providing a template for accelerating other numerical routines in real-time systems.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of structured matrix methods from numerical analysis with hardware-aware memory architecture design, specifically tailored for control-theoretic computations, is a non-obvious synthesis beyond the current state-of-the-art which typically treats algorithm and memory optimization separately.

proposals.title: A Unified Framework for Stability and Performance Analysis of Multi-Rate Sampled Control Systems
proposals.motivation: Modern cyber-physical systems, such as networked control systems and integrated sensor fusion platforms, inherently operate with multiple, asynchronous sampling rates. Pre-2026 analysis tools, including the Relay Autotuner and standard LTI frameworks, are fundamentally limited for these heterogeneous systems, leading to conservative designs or undetected instability. Extending analysis to multi-rate systems is critical for the robust design of next-generation automation and medical devices where different physiological or physical processes are sampled at different frequencies.
proposals.hypothesis: By constructing a unified, lifted time-invariant model for multi-rate sampled-data systems, it is possible to perform rigorous stability and performance analysis using established frequency-domain and Lyapunov methods, thereby enabling the application of advanced autotuning and optimization techniques to these heterogeneous systems.
proposals.method: We will extend the existing single-rate analysis package by implementing a lifting technique to transform the periodic multi-rate system into an equivalent, higher-dimensional linear time-invariant discrete-time model. This lifted model will serve as the core for analysis algorithms, including the computation of structured singular values (mu-analysis) for robustness assessment and the application of convex optimization for controller synthesis. The implementation will leverage efficient, reconfigurable memory architectures, akin to those used for real-time feature extraction, to handle the increased dimensionality of the lifted state-space matrices.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical software tool for the analysis and design of multi-rate control systems, bridging a significant gap between theory and application. It will enable more efficient and less conservative controller design for complex systems found in automotive, robotics, and medical technology, directly supporting the development of advanced, multi-sensor diagnostic and control platforms.
proposals.novelty: While lifting is a known theoretical concept, its integration into a practical, accessible analysis package tailored for control engineers, combined with computational methods informed by real-time memory management for high-dimensional systems, represents a novel and non-obvious engineering contribution.

proposals.title: Dual Adaptive Control via Memory-Augmented Neural Networks for Systems with Latent Parameter Drift
proposals.motivation: In critical applications like adaptive drug infusion or real-time process control, system parameters are often assumed known or slowly varying. However, in complex physiological or mechanical systems, parameters can undergo sudden, unobserved shifts (e.g., due to a changing patient state or component wear), degrading the performance of standard adaptive controllers and estimators. Pre-2026 methods often separate identification and control, creating a fragility to concurrent state and parameter uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: A control architecture that jointly learns a latent representation of time-varying system parameters and directly maps this representation to control actions will outperform traditional dual control and adaptive schemes in the presence of abrupt, unmeasured parameter changes.
proposals.method: We will develop a recurrent neural network (RNN) with a differentiable external memory module, inspired by Neural Turing Machines, to serve as an adaptive observer. This network will process the error between predicted and observed system outputs to maintain a persistent, evolving memory of inferred parameter dynamics. The memory state will be fed into a policy network, trained via deep reinforcement learning (e.g., DDPG or PPO), to output control signals. The system will be validated on a high-fidelity simulator of prostate artery hemodynamics, where vessel compliance (parameter) changes mimic disease progression, and on benchmark relay autotuner problems with injected faults.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven framework for dual adaptive control that explicitly addresses the challenge of latent parameter drift. It will yield a practical control algorithm that reduces the need for persistent excitation solely for identification and demonstrates improved robustness on biomedical and industrial benchmarks. The project will also contribute a reusable, reconfigurable memory architecture for real-time parameter tracking in control systems.
proposals.novelty: While Åström's autotuners separate identification and control, this method merges them into a single, end-to-learnable architecture using an explicit, manipulable memory for parameter tracking. This differs from standard adaptive RNNs by providing an inspectable and structured memory of parameter dynamics, enabling more stable and interpretable adaptation.

proposals.title: A Generalized Filtering Framework for Nonlinear Systems with State-Dependent Noise and Hybrid Dynamics
proposals.motivation: State estimation for nonlinear systems remains a core challenge in control and signal processing. While filters like the Extended and Unscented Kalman Filters (EKF/UKF) are standard, they assume additive Gaussian noise and smooth dynamics, limiting their application to critical real-world systems like biomedical processes (e.g., pulsatile blood flow) or fault-prone mechatronics, which exhibit discontinuous transitions and noise dependent on the system state.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating tools from stochastic hybrid systems and leveraging recent advances in numerical integration and automatic differentiation, a unified filtering algorithm can be developed that provides consistent and computationally tractable estimates for a broader class of nonlinear systems, including those with non-additive (state-dependent) noise and discontinuous jumps in dynamics.
proposals.method: The method will extend the UKF paradigm by formulating a generalized sigma-point selection and propagation scheme that accounts for state-dependent noise covariance. For discontinuous dynamics, we will model the system as a stochastic hybrid automaton, employing a multiple-model filtering approach where mode probabilities are updated based on residual analysis. Key algorithms will include a modified UKF with propagated noise sigma points and an Interacting Multiple Model (IMM) estimator, implemented and validated using tools like CasADi for algorithmic differentiation to handle Jacobian-free propagation of complex noise models.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, practical filtering algorithm with open-source implementation, directly applicable to problems in biomedical engineering (e.g., modeling arterial pressure with turbulent flow) and fault-tolerant control. It will formally extend nonlinear estimation theory to more general system classes, bridging a gap between theoretical stochastic hybrid systems and applied filtering.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of sigma-point methods with hybrid system models to handle both state-dependent noise and discontinuities simultaneously within a single, cohesive filtering framework, moving beyond the common assumption of additive noise or purely continuous dynamics.

proposals.title: A Reconfigurable Memory Architecture and Low-Rank Factorization for Scalable Kalman Filtering in High-Dimensional Medical Imaging Systems
proposals.motivation: The application of Kalman filters and related Bayesian estimators to high-dimensional systems, such as real-time 3D medical image reconstruction or physiological signal tracking, is computationally prohibitive due to O(n^3) complexity in state dimension. This limits their use in next-generation diagnostic tools, like the myocardial perfusion analysis referenced in recent work, where rapid, high-fidelity filtering could enhance detection of conditions like coronary artery disease. Efficient implementations are critical for deploying advanced estimation algorithms on embedded or real-time clinical hardware.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by exploiting inherent low-rank structure in the error covariance matrices of high-dimensional physiological systems and implementing a reconfigurable, multi-access pattern vector memory architecture, the computational and memory burden of the Kalman filter update can be reduced by an order of magnitude without significant loss of estimation fidelity.
proposals.method: The approach merges numerical linear algebra with hardware-aware algorithm design. First, we will develop a truncated Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) or Randomized Kaczmarz method to maintain a low-rank factorization of the covariance matrix, reducing update complexity. Second, inspired by Åström's recent work on vector memories for feature extraction, we will design a reconfigurable memory controller optimized for the irregular, data-dependent access patterns of this factorized filter update. The system will be prototyped and validated on a high-dimensional state-space model derived from dynamic SPECT or MRI cardiac imaging sequences.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, hardware-efficient algorithmic framework for high-dimensional Kalman filtering, directly applicable to real-time medical imaging systems. It will provide open-source VHDL/Verilog cores for the memory architecture and benchmark software demonstrating order-of-magnitude speed-ups on representative clinical data models, bridging a gap between advanced estimation theory and practical implementation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of a low-rank numerical factorization specifically for the structured noise models of medical systems with a reconfigurable memory architecture, an integration not explored in prior literature. This moves beyond generic model reduction to a full system optimization tailored for the access patterns of the proposed algorithm.

proposals.title: Data-Driven Filter Synthesis via Subspace Identification for Real-Time Medical Signal Processing
proposals.motivation: In medical diagnostics, such as analyzing myocardial perfusion scintigraphy or arterial waveforms, filters are often designed based on idealized models that may not capture patient-specific or time-varying system dynamics. This can limit the accuracy of feature extraction for conditions like coronary artery disease. Leveraging Åström's legacy in system identification, there is a significant opportunity to create adaptive filters directly from observed time-series data, improving real-time signal processing in clinical tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying subspace identification methods (e.g., NASID, PBSID) to clinical time-series data will yield high-fidelity, low-order state-space models that can be directly synthesized into digital filters, outperforming conventional fixed-filter designs in terms of signal-to-noise ratio and feature preservation.
proposals.method: The approach will utilize pre-2026 subspace identification algorithms to estimate state-space models from multi-channel medical time-series data (e.g., scintigraphy sequences or arterial pressure waveforms). The identified models will be converted to IIR or FIR filter structures via balanced truncation and Tustin transformation. Implementation will target a reconfigurable vector memory architecture, similar to prior ORB feature extraction work, to enable real-time, adaptive filter updates. Performance will be validated against deep neural network benchmarks from recent perfusion analysis papers.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodology for synthesizing adaptive filters directly from data, bridging the gap between system identification theory and real-time medical signal processing. It will provide a computationally efficient alternative to end-to-end black-box neural networks, offering interpretable models and reliable performance in resource-constrained clinical systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct synthesis of implementable filter coefficients from data-driven subspace models, moving beyond classical autotuning of controller parameters to the autotuning of signal processing filters themselves, specifically for non-stationary biomedical signals.

proposals.title: Memory-Constrained Relay Autotuning: A Subspace Identification Approach for Microcontroller-Based Systems
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of micro-systems in IoT and medical devices (e.g., implantable sensors, micro-pumps) creates a demand for adaptive control under severe memory and processing constraints. Classical autotuners, including relay-based methods pioneered by Åström, often require computational resources unavailable on these platforms, limiting their autonomy and performance. This work addresses the gap between adaptive control theory and the reality of ultra-low-power embedded hardware.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a memory-optimal subspace identification algorithm with a next-generation relay autotuning framework, it is possible to derive and implement high-performance, adaptive PID controllers for systems with kilobytes of RAM and no floating-point unit.
proposals.method: We will develop a tailored Numerical Algorithms for Subspace State-System Identification (N4SID) variant that operates on fixed-point arithmetic and exploits the reconfigurable vector memory architectures from recent ORB feature extraction research to manage matrix sketches. This identification core will be embedded within a modified relay feedback experiment, where the excitation signal and data capture are co-designed to minimize memory footprint. The implementation will be validated on a microcontroller platform simulating a micro-fluidic drug delivery system, a relevant 'micro-system' analog.
proposals.contribution: This will yield a fully documented, open-source autotuning package for memory-constrained devices, extending Åström's relay autotuning legacy to the micro-system domain. It will provide a concrete methodology for trading identification accuracy against RAM/ROM usage, offering a new design paradigm for embedded control engineers.
proposals.novelty: The novel synthesis of fixed-point subspace identification with relay autotuning for microcontrollers is unexplored. Unlike simply simplifying existing algorithms, this approach co-designs the identification and excitation phases around a hardware-aware memory architecture.

proposals.title: A Reconfigurable Multi-Access Memory Architecture for Decentralized Consensus Kalman Filtering in Distributed Sensor Networks
proposals.motivation: The scaling of Kalman filtering to large, distributed sensor networks, such as those in environmental monitoring or smart infrastructure, is hampered by communication bottlenecks and computational complexity. Centralized fusion is infeasible, and existing decentralized methods often lack robustness to network partitions and dynamic reconfiguration. This work addresses the critical need for scalable, real-time state estimation in spatially extensive systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a decentralized Kalman filter, enhanced with a consensus protocol and supported by a reconfigurable multi-access pattern vector memory architecture, will achieve near-optimal estimation performance with bounded communication overhead and inherent robustness to network topology changes.
proposals.method: We will develop a decentralized Information Filter formulation, where local sensors perform prediction and update steps. A consensus algorithm, specifically the Metropolis weights method, will be used to iteratively align local information vectors and matrices over a communication graph. The core innovation is a hardware-inspired, reconfigurable vector memory unit (akin to Åström's recent ORB feature extractor work) to manage the multiple access patterns (e.g., sequential, strided) required for efficient storage and retrieval of covariance blocks and consensus message buffers in real-time.
proposals.contribution: This research will yield a novel, implementable algorithm-hardware co-design for distributed estimation, directly extending Åström's legacy in autotuning and real-time systems. It will provide a formal analysis of stability and convergence rates under communication constraints and demonstrate a significant reduction in latency and bandwidth usage compared to baseline distributed Kalman filters on simulated large-scale network benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a consensus protocol with a reconfigurable memory architecture tailored for the specific dataflow patterns of decentralized filtering is novel. This moves beyond purely algorithmic approaches to a system-level solution that explicitly manages the 'memory' and 'tool' constraints of real-time, distributed sensing.

proposals.title: Inverse Optimal Control for Implicit Cost Inference in Biological and Engineered Systems
proposals.motivation: Inverse optimal control (IOC) is a fundamental problem for understanding and replicating intelligent behavior, both in engineered systems and biological processes. Pre-2026, while IOC methods exist, they often struggle with high-dimensional, noisy observations and non-convex cost structures, limiting application to complex real-world systems like biological regulatory networks or adaptive control loops. This gap hinders the ability to reverse-engineer the implicit objectives behind observed control laws, a critical step for explainable AI and biomimetic design.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating the IOC problem within a flexible, memory-augmented neural network framework, we can robustly infer the underlying cost function from a sequence of observed state-control pairs, even when the true cost is non-quadratic and the dynamics are partially unknown.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep neural network architecture with a dedicated memory module, inspired by recent work on reconfigurable vector memories for real-time feature extraction, to learn a mapping from observed trajectories to cost function parameters. The method will integrate a differentiable simulation layer, akin to those used in relay autotuning analysis, to forward-propagate candidate costs. Training will employ a contrastive learning objective, comparing the observed control law against policies generated by solving the forward optimal control problem for inferred costs, using efficient solvers like iLQR.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a general, data-driven framework for IOC that is applicable to systems with complex, non-Markovian dynamics, such as those encountered in physiological regulation (e.g., cardiovascular or cancer treatment models). It will advance the field beyond linear-quadratic assumptions and offer a new tool for analyzing biological control strategies and refining autonomous system design.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a learned, structured memory component within an IOC framework to handle partial observability and long-horizon dependencies is novel. This approach moves beyond standard regression-based IOC by explicitly modeling the temporal context in which control decisions are made.

proposals.title: Online Recursive Identification of Time-Varying Physiological Systems via Adaptive Kalman Filtering
proposals.motivation: Many physiological systems, such as cardiovascular dynamics or glucose regulation, exhibit time-varying parameters due to disease progression, therapeutic intervention, or circadian rhythms. Pre-2026 system identification methods often assume time-invariance, limiting their applicability in real-time monitoring and adaptive control for personalized medicine. A recursive, online method that provides parameter estimates with confidence bounds is crucial for developing next-generation diagnostic tools and closed-loop therapeutic systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That the parameters of a linear time-varying system can be accurately tracked online by formulating them as states in an augmented dynamic model and estimated using an adaptive Kalman filter, analogous to navigation state estimation for a vehicle with changing dynamics.
proposals.method: We will augment the state vector of a linear system model to include its time-varying parameters, modeling their evolution as a stochastic process (e.g., a random walk). An Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) or a Dual Kalman Filter will be implemented for the joint estimation of states and parameters. The algorithm will be validated on synthetic data and applied to real pre-2026 clinical datasets, such as arterial blood pressure waveforms or prostate-specific antigen time series, where underlying system dynamics may shift due to pathological changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust, online algorithm for tracking time-varying system parameters with inherent uncertainty quantification. It will provide a formal framework for recursive identification in biomedical engineering, moving beyond batch processing. The method could enhance real-time feature extraction for conditions like obstructive coronary artery disease by modeling the time-varying nature of cardiac response.
