proposals.novelty: The explicit analogy and transfer of the adaptive Kalman filtering paradigm from aerospace navigation to biomedical system identification is novel. It directly inverts the core assumption of time-invariance in classical identification, offering a recursive solution with confidence bounds, unlike windowed or forgetting-factor approaches.

proposals.title: Expectation-Maximization for System Identification with Gappy Data: A Data Assimilation Framework
proposals.motivation: System identification traditionally requires complete, uniformly sampled input-output sequences, a condition often violated in real-world applications like biomedical monitoring (e.g., irregularly sampled physiological signals) or networked control systems with packet loss. This limitation hinders the application of robust model-based control and analysis. The field lacks principled methods to identify dynamical models directly from fragmented, irregularly sampled time-series data without resorting to simplistic interpolation.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating system identification with incomplete data as a latent variable problem, an Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm, inspired by data assimilation techniques in paleoclimatology, can jointly impute missing data points and identify an accurate system model, converging to a maximum-likelihood estimate.
proposals.method: We will develop an EM algorithm where the E-step performs probabilistic imputation of missing inputs, outputs, or states. This will use a Kalman filter/smoother (for linear models) or a particle smoother (for nonlinear cases) conditioned on the current model parameters and all observed data fragments. The M-step will then execute a standard prediction-error identification (e.g., using a state-space model parameterization) on the complete, imputed dataset. The algorithm will be initialized using a model identified from linearly interpolated data and iterated until convergence of the log-likelihood.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, general-purpose algorithm for identifying dynamical systems from gappy data, expanding the applicability of system identification to new domains with inherent measurement limitations. It will formally bridge concepts from statistical data assimilation and control theory, offering a practical tool with demonstrated performance on benchmarks and a case study using irregularly sampled biomedical signals.
proposals.novelty: While EM is known in system identification for handling latent states (e.g., hidden Markov models), its direct application to impute missing *observed* variables (inputs/outputs) during the identification process is novel. The explicit analogy and adaptation of paleoclimate proxy-record assimilation techniques to this core control problem represents a significant conceptual crossover.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Framework for Joint System Identification and Trajectory Reconstruction Under Missing Data and Errors-in-Variables
proposals.motivation: In practical applications like biomedical signal processing and networked control, input-output data sequences are often fragmented due to sensor dropouts or occlusions, and are simultaneously corrupted by measurement noise. Traditional identification methods, which assume either complete data or noise-free anchor points, produce biased models under these joint conditions, leading to unreliable predictions and poor controller design.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating a unified Bayesian inference problem that treats missing trajectory segments as latent variables, it is possible to derive statistically consistent model estimates and reconstruct the complete, denoised input-output sequences, even when large portions of data are absent.
proposals.method: We will develop an Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm where the E-step imputes the missing data and noise sequences using a Kalman smoother conditioned on the current model estimate and structured priors (e.g., smoothness constraints). The M-step updates the system model parameters via prediction error minimization. The core innovation is a tailored, reconfigurable memory architecture—inspired by recent work on vector memories for feature extraction—to efficiently manage and constrain the high-dimensional latent state of the missing trajectory during optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, computationally tractable algorithm for robust identification from corrupted and incomplete records. It will extend the theoretical understanding of identifiability in errors-in-variables problems with missing data, offering a practical tool for domains like medical diagnostics, where clean, continuous signals are rare.
proposals.novelty: The integration of trajectory reconstruction as an integral part of the identification loss function, managed via a structured latent variable model and a dedicated memory architecture, moves beyond the standard two-step approach of imputation-then-identification, which is known to propagate errors.

proposals.title: Joint Input-Output Denoising and System Identification via Geophysical Blind Deconvolution Priors
proposals.motivation: Errors-in-variables (EIV) system identification, where both input and output measurements are corrupted by noise, remains a fundamental challenge in control and signal processing. Accurate models are critical for robust control design, especially in biomedical applications like cardiovascular system modeling where signals are inherently noisy. Existing EIV methods often rely on restrictive assumptions or lack mechanisms to incorporate domain-specific knowledge, limiting their practical utility.
proposals.hypothesis: By reformulating the EIV problem as a blind deconvolution task analogous to seismic signal processing, and by imposing application-specific physical or statistical priors on the latent noise-free input, we can achieve more accurate and robust joint estimation of the system dynamics and the denoised input sequence.
proposals.method: We will develop a probabilistic framework where the noise-free input is a latent variable with a prior distribution (e.g., sparsity-promoting Laplacian or structured autoregressive models inspired by physiological signals). System parameters will be modeled with Gaussian process or sparse Bayesian learning priors. We will implement joint estimation using variational inference, constructing a mean-field approximation to approximate the posterior distribution of the latent input and system parameters, enabling efficient computation and uncertainty quantification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, flexible algorithmic framework for EIV identification that explicitly incorporates prior knowledge, moving beyond traditional correlation-based methods. It will deliver not only a system model but also a denoised estimate of the input signal, enhancing interpretability. The methodology will be validated on benchmark problems and real data from biomedical system identification, such as modeling the arterial tree from noisy pressure and flow measurements.
proposals.novelty: The direct adaptation of geophysical blind deconvolution concepts and priors to dynamic system identification is novel. The integration of structured, application-driven priors within a variational EIV framework offers a significant departure from classical instrumental variable or total least squares approaches.

proposals.title: Co-Designed Robust Identification and Nussbaum-Gain Control for Errors-in-Variables Systems with Unknown High-Frequency Gain
proposals.motivation: In many biomedical and mechatronic systems, such as robotic-assisted surgery or drug infusion, the true actuator command is inferred from noisy sensors and the plant's gain sign and magnitude are initially unknown. Standard adaptive control fails with input noise (errors-in-variables), while robust identification alone does not resolve the control direction problem. A unified framework is needed for safe, data-driven control in these high-uncertainty scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a set-membership or covariance-matching errors-in-variables identifier with a Nussbaum-gain-based adaptive law, one can achieve stable closed-loop control for linear time-invariant systems where both the input and output are corrupted by noise and the high-frequency gain is completely unknown.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage algorithm. First, a Frisch scheme or recursive errors-in-variables estimator will provide bounded parameter sets, accounting for correlated input-output noise. Second, these sets will inform a novel Nussbaum-type controller, where the gain exploration is constrained by the identifier's confidence intervals, reducing erratic switching. Stability will be analyzed using averaging and singular perturbation theory for the coupled identifier-controller dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first provably stable adaptive control algorithm for errors-in-variables systems with unknown control direction. It will provide a practical tool for controlling systems with significant actuation uncertainty, bridging the gap between robust identification and direction-seeking adaptive control.
proposals.novelty: The co-design of the identifier and controller is key; existing methods treat these problems separately. Using the identifier's uncertainty quantification to govern the Nussbaum gain's search is a novel synthesis of set-membership identification and adaptive stabilization.

proposals.title: A Regularized Instrumental Variable Approach for Errors-in-Variables Identification using Deep Feature Encoders
proposals.motivation: Errors-in-variables (EIV) identification is crucial for accurate modeling in fields like biomedical signal processing and relay autotuning, where input measurements (e.g., stimuli, control signals) are also corrupted by noise. Pre-2026, many EIV methods rely on strong assumptions about noise distributions or require specific experimental designs, limiting their robustness in practical, data-rich applications with complex noise structures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel identification framework combining the statistical consistency of instrumental variable methods with the feature extraction power of deep neural networks can yield robust parameter estimates under general EIV conditions without restrictive noise assumptions.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage algorithm. First, a convolutional encoder, inspired by architectures used in pre-2026 perfusion imaging analysis, will extract a low-dimensional, noise-reduced feature sequence from the noisy input observations to serve as instruments. Second, a regularized instrumental variable criterion, penalizing model complexity akin to modern autotuner principles, will be optimized. The implementation will leverage reconfigurable memory architectures for efficient real-time feature extraction, similar to those used for ORB features, to handle streaming data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a general-purpose, computationally efficient EIV identification tool that reduces bias for a wider class of systems and noise scenarios. It will bridge modern deep learning-based feature engineering with classical system identification theory, offering a practical advance for applications in medical device calibration and industrial control.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious synthesis lies in using a learned deep feature encoder, rather than pre-defined external signals, to generate statistically valid instruments specifically tailored to mitigate input noise, combined with a regularization framework informed by Åström's autotuning legacy.

proposals.title: Regime-Aware Bootstrap Selection for Robust MIMO Model Order Identification in Finite Data Regimes
proposals.motivation: Model order selection for MIMO systems using asymptotic criteria like AIC or BIC is unreliable with short, noisy, or closed-loop operational data, common in biomedical and industrial applications. This leads to models that fit the training data but fail to generalize or guarantee stability, compromising control performance and diagnostic reliability. A finite-sample, stability-aware selection framework is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: A model order selection criterion based on a direct estimate of finite-sample generalization error, computed via a regime-aware block bootstrap that preserves temporal correlation and operational phases, will yield more robust and stable MIMO models than traditional asymptotic information criteria, especially for short data records.
proposals.method: We will develop a regime-aware block bootstrap procedure that segments time-series data into distinct operational phases (e.g., normal, transient, fault) using change-point detection, then resamples blocks within phases to preserve local dynamics. For each candidate model order/structure, parameters are estimated on multiple resampled datasets. The selection criterion combines the empirical fit loss with a stability penalty derived from the variance of the pole locations of the estimated models across bootstrap replicates, directly penalizing instability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, data-driven tool for system identification that explicitly prioritizes model stability and generalization, moving beyond asymptotic theory. It will enable more reliable modeling from limited datasets in fields like medical diagnostics (e.g., cardiovascular system modeling) and adaptive control, where robust models are essential.
proposals.novelty: The method replaces asymptotic penalties with a finite-sample, computationally estimated stability penalty, and uniquely integrates regime-aware resampling to handle non-stationary operational data, a significant departure from standard bootstrap model selection.

proposals.title: Robust Information Criteria for MIMO System Identification: A Regularization and Cross-Validation Approach
proposals.motivation: Classical information criteria like AIC and BIC are foundational for model order selection but can be unreliable for complex, high-dimensional MIMO systems, especially with limited or noisy data prevalent in biomedical and industrial applications. The proliferation of deep learning for system analysis, as seen in recent diagnostic networks, underscores the need for principled, automated model structure selection that generalizes well beyond training data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that augmenting traditional information criteria with data-driven regularization penalties and structured cross-validation will yield a more robust and consistent selection mechanism for MIMO model order and structure, particularly for systems with correlated inputs and outputs.
proposals.method: The method will extend the generalized information criterion (GIC) framework by integrating two key components. First, we will derive problem-specific regularization terms inspired by Gleason scoring and multi-access pattern memory architectures to penalize model complexity in a structured way, promoting sparsity and interpretability. Second, we will design a multi-fold cross-validation scheme, leveraging concepts from relay autotuning, to evaluate model predictive performance on dynamically partitioned validation data, moving beyond pure likelihood-based penalties. The approach will be validated on benchmark MIMO datasets and real clinical time-series data related to cardiovascular dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, more reliable criterion for automated MIMO model selection, directly applicable to data-driven modeling in control and medical diagnostics. It will offer a principled alternative to ad-hoc selection in neural network-based system identification, enhancing model trustworthiness and generalizability.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of statistical information theory with data-driven regularization patterns from unrelated domains (e.g., Gleason scoring) and a validation strategy informed by autotuning concepts, creating a hybrid criterion tailored for modern, complex system identification problems.

proposals.title: Asymptotic Analysis of Adaptive Control via Sparse System Identification and Stochastic Approximation
proposals.motivation: Classical adaptive control theory relies on the critical and often unrealistic assumption that the plant order is known a priori. Modern data-driven methods in system identification, such as sparse regression (e.g., LASSO) and variational Bayesian inference, can estimate model order from data, but their integration into adaptive feedback loops lacks a rigorous asymptotic performance theory. This gap limits the formal application of adaptive control to complex systems with latent or evolving structures, such as those encountered in biomedical applications (e.g., cardiovascular dynamics) where model dimensionality may be uncertain.
proposals.hypothesis: By coupling a recursive stochastic approximation-based adaptive controller with a sparse online identification algorithm featuring consistent order selection, the joint parameter-and-order estimation process converges almost surely, leading to a stable closed-loop system with asymptotic performance guarantees comparable to the case of known order.
proposals.method: The core technical approach is to extend the ODE method for stochastic approximations to analyze the interconnection of a certainty-equivalence adaptive controller (e.g., Minimum Variance or Pole Placement) with an online recursive identification algorithm employing ℓ1-regularization or variational approximations for automatic relevance determination. We will model the joint evolution of parameter estimates and a dimensionality indicator as a mixed-time-scale stochastic process. The associated limiting ODE will be derived, and its equilibrium points, corresponding to the true system order and parameters, will be analyzed for stability using Lyapunov methods, drawing on pre-2026 convergence results for sparse estimators.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified asymptotic convergence theory for adaptive control with order-agnostic identification, delivering formal stability and performance bounds. It will establish a theoretical foundation for applying modern sparse and Bayesian identification techniques in real-time control, directly impacting fields like biomedical engineering where model complexity is not fixed.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the asymptotic analysis of the coupled dynamics of controller and order-estimating identifier, moving beyond the classical setting of fixed, known system order to provide guarantees for a joint estimation-of-structure-and-parameters process in closed loop.

proposals.title: Cross-Validated Recursive Design: Data-Driven Structure Selection for Passivity-Based Adaptive Control
proposals.motivation: Recursive control synthesis methods like adaptive backstepping provide stability guarantees but require an a priori, fixed model structure, which is often uncertain in practice. Conversely, purely data-driven methods lack formal stability assurances. This gap limits the robust and efficient automation of control design for complex systems, such as interconnected robotic manipulators or power converters, where model order is not precisely known.
proposals.hypothesis: That a recursive passivity-based control law, whose structural complexity (e.g., number of recursive steps, interconnection terms) is selected via data-driven regularization and cross-validation, will achieve comparable or superior performance and robustness to a fixed high-order design while being more parsimonious and data-efficient.
proposals.method: We will formulate the recursive control design as a nested model selection problem. An outer loop will use k-fold cross-validation on operational data to evaluate candidate structures defined by recursion depth and interconnection topology. An inner synthesis loop will employ regularized least-squares or sparse regression (e.g., LASSO) within the parameter update laws of an adaptive backstepping or interconnection-and-damping-assignment passivity-based control (IDA-PBC) algorithm to prune insignificant terms. Stability will be proven by showing the selected structure preserves a passivity property for the interconnected system.
proposals.contribution: A novel adaptive control synthesis framework that formally integrates statistical learning for model selection with stability-guaranteed recursive design. This will yield a principled method for trading off controller complexity against performance from data, directly applicable to systems with uncertain model order, such as flexible-joint robots or multi-converter power systems.
proposals.novelty: The integration of cross-validation and regularization directly into the recursive *structure-building* process of passivity-based control, moving beyond parameter tuning to learn the stabilizing interconnection topology itself, which is non-trivial given the need to preserve passivity at each selection step.

proposals.title: Spectral Relay Autotuning: Frequency-Domain System Identification for Multi-Loop Relay Feedback Networks
proposals.motivation: Relay autotuning, pioneered by Åström and Hägglund, is a cornerstone of industrial PID control, but its classical time-domain analysis struggles with complex, multi-loop systems exhibiting significant noise or load disturbances. Recent work on next-generation relay autotuners seeks improved robustness and configurability. Simultaneously, advanced spectral estimation techniques from signal processing offer powerful tools for non-parametric frequency response identification under noisy conditions, yet their integration into core autotuning algorithms remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: By employing modern spectral estimation methods (e.g., Welch's method, multitaper, or subspace-based techniques) to analyze the oscillatory signals in a reconfigurable multi-loop relay experiment, we can more accurately and robustly identify a system's critical frequency-response points (phase and gain crossover) for controller design, even in high-noise environments typical of biomedical or process control applications.
proposals.method: We will design a reconfigurable experimental framework where standard and modified relay feedback loops are applied to a system. Instead of relying solely on describing function analysis of time-domain oscillation amplitude/period, we will process the input-output signals using spectral methods to estimate the system's frequency response. Specifically, we will implement and compare Welch's averaged periodogram for noise reduction and the ESPRIT algorithm for high-resolution frequency estimation. This spectral data will directly feed into rules for calculating PID parameters, extending the classical Ziegler-Nichols relations. The method will be validated on simulated models of arterial blood pressure dynamics and prostate cancer cell growth, systems with known challenging dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, robust frequency-domain identification core for next-generation autotuners, reducing their sensitivity to noise and disturbances. It will provide a formal bridge between classical control autotuning and modern signal processing spectral analysis, with validated performance on physiologically relevant models. The outcome will be a publishable algorithm and analysis framework for spectral relay autotuning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct replacement of time-domain describing function analysis in relay feedback with advanced, non-parametric spectral estimation techniques, creating a hybrid 'spectral relay' method. This approach is non-obvious as it reinterprets the core identification mechanism of a well-established tool through a modern signal processing lens, specifically targeting the noise robustness challenges noted in recent autotuning research.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical Adaptive Relay Autotuner with Confidence-Based Mode Switching for Safety-Critical Embedded Control
proposals.motivation: The deployment of adaptive control and system identification in safety-critical embedded systems, such as medical devices or autonomous robots, is hampered by the inherent tension between computational efficiency and robust performance under uncertainty. Lightweight algorithms like relay autotuners are attractive for real-time use but can fail catastrophically with noisy, ambiguous, or out-of-distribution process data. A framework that guarantees a minimum level of functional safety and graceful performance degradation is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical identification architecture, combining a fast primary identifier, a real-time confidence estimator, and an on-demand robust verifier, can maintain core safety guarantees and stable operation under uncertainty, where a single static model would fail.
proposals.method: The method extends Åström's relay autotuning framework. A primary, lightweight recursive estimator (e.g., a modified RLS algorithm) runs continuously. A parallel confidence estimator, inspired by techniques in real-time feature extraction (e.g., ORB descriptor matching quality) and medical AI uncertainty quantification (e.g., from the listed perfusion scintigraphy paper), monitors residual characteristics and innovation sequences. Upon low-confidence detection, the system triggers a mode switch: either invoking a more robust but computationally intensive verifier (e.g., a set-membership identification routine) or executing a pre-computed, conservative fail-safe policy stored in a reconfigurable memory structure. The switching logic itself will be formally analyzed for stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formally analyzed design pattern for adaptive identification in safety-critical embedded systems, ensuring graceful degradation. It will deliver a concrete implementation framework, merging concepts from adaptive control, real-time systems, and robust AI, with validation on a representative embedded platform.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of real-time confidence monitoring and verifiable safe-mode switching into the classical adaptive control identification loop, creating a hybrid system that explicitly manages the trade-off between performance and safety under computational constraints.

proposals.title: Compressed Sensing for Real-Time System Identification: A Sparsity-Driven Approach to Lightweight Model Learning
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of embedded and edge devices in control and monitoring applications demands system identification methods that are computationally lightweight and data-efficient. Traditional identification techniques are often too resource-intensive for real-time execution on platforms with strict memory, power, and latency constraints. Drawing an analogy from medical MRI, where compressed sensing revolutionized data acquisition by exploiting inherent sparsity, this research addresses the analogous problem of minimizing excitation and computational burden in identifying systems for embedded control.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating the system identification problem within a compressed sensing framework, we can develop algorithms that accurately reconstruct parsimonious dynamic models from significantly undersampled temporal data, enabling feasible real-time identification on resource-constrained hardware.
proposals.method: We will develop an identification pipeline where the excitation signal is designed as a sparse, pseudo-random sequence to maximally inform a predefined overcomplete dictionary (e.g., Laguerre basis or wavelet frames). The core algorithm will be a modified Orthogonal Matching Pursuit (OMP) or LASSO solver, optimized for embedded execution through fixed-point arithmetic and leveraging the researcher's expertise in reconfigurable vector memory architectures. The resulting sparse parameter vector will directly define a low-complexity model (e.g., a pruned ARX structure or a compact state-space representation), validated on benchmark systems including thermal and electromechanical loops.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel system identification methodology that explicitly trades off model accuracy against data acquisition and computational cost, a formalization previously lacking in the embedded control literature. It will provide concrete algorithms and implementation strategies for deploying adaptive tuning and real-time model learning on microcontrollers and FPGAs, extending the principles of relay autotuners to more complex, sparse model structures.
proposals.novelty: The direct translation of compressed sensing principles from medical imaging to the core problem of dynamic system identification is novel. The integration of sparsity-seeking reconstruction with embedded-optimized numerical solvers and memory architectures creates a unique, co-designed approach for lightweight learning.

proposals.title: Co-Design of Stabilizing Control and Real-Time Parameter Experimentation for Resource-Constrained Embedded Systems
proposals.motivation: Embedded control systems in medical devices, robotics, and automotive applications require adaptive models for fault detection and performance optimization, but must operate under severe computational and memory constraints. Traditional online system identification methods are computationally heavy and treat experiment design as a separate, offline process, making them unsuitable for real-time embedded use. A unified framework for lightweight, active learning within a stabilizing controller is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-designing the stabilizing feedback controller and the parameter estimation experiment as a single constrained optimization problem, it is possible to generate minimal, persistently informative control signals that enable real-time parameter learning while strictly guaranteeing stability and respecting fixed, limited computational budgets.
proposals.method: We will extend the theory of relay autotuners and experiment design to a real-time, codesigned setting. The core algorithm will formulate a receding-horizon optimal control problem where the cost function balances regulation performance with an information metric (e.g., Fisher information) for parameter estimation. To enforce strict resource limits, we will integrate a reconfigurable vector memory architecture, similar to those used for real-time feature extraction, to manage the streaming data and parameter updates efficiently. Stability will be enforced via Lyapunov-based constraints within the optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal methodology and practical implementation for safe, real-time parameter learning in embedded control. It will deliver a novel class of adaptive controllers that can perform online model refinement and early fault detection in systems where traditional identification is impossible. The results will be demonstrated on a benchmark problem relevant to biomedical systems, such as a simplified model of arterial pressure dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The tight integration of active experiment design into the real-time control law under explicit computational and memory constraints is a significant departure from classical system identification. This moves beyond treating identification as a separate, batch process, enabling truly concurrent learning and control in severely resource-limited hardware.

proposals.title: Lightweight, Memory-Efficient System Identification for Embedded Control Using Pruned Neural Networks and Recursive Projection Algorithms
proposals.motivation: The deployment of advanced control and diagnostic algorithms, such as autotuners and medical image classifiers, on embedded and real-time hardware is hindered by the computational and memory footprint of modern identification methods. Existing deep learning models and recursive identification algorithms often exceed the constraints of microcontrollers and FPGAs used in industrial and medical devices. A systematic methodology to scale down these algorithms is urgently needed to enable intelligent, adaptive functionality at the edge.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-designing neural network pruning techniques with recursive projection-based identification algorithms, it is possible to create a unified, lightweight identification framework that maintains essential accuracy while meeting the strict timing and memory budgets of embedded real-time systems.
proposals.method: The research will develop a two-pronged approach. First, for parametric models, we will adapt and optimize the Recursive Projection Algorithm (RPA), a core Åström autotuning method, by implementing fixed-point arithmetic and a reconfigurable vector memory architecture to manage states efficiently. Second, for non-parametric or feature-based identification (inspired by diagnostic networks), we will apply structured and iterative pruning to pre-trained compact networks (like MobileNetV2) based on hardware-aware metrics, followed by quantization-aware training. Both strands will be validated on benchmark problems: RPA on a real-time motor control testbed, and pruned networks on the task of extracting ORB features or classifying ECG patterns for embedded medical sensing.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a set of concrete, open-source software blocks for resource-constrained system identification, bridging the gap between control theory and embedded machine learning. It will provide quantitative performance trade-off curves (accuracy vs. latency vs. memory) for these scaled-down methods, offering practitioners a clear guide for implementation. The outcome directly supports the next generation of adaptive industrial relays and portable diagnostic devices.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic application of hardware-conscious neural network pruning from computer vision to the domain of dynamic system identification and control, combined with the low-level optimization of foundational recursive estimation algorithms like RPA. This creates a cross-disciplinary methodology for algorithm scaling not previously systematized in the control literature.

proposals.title: Optimal Input Design for Physiological System Identification via Reinforcement Learning and Multi-Access Memory Architectures
proposals.motivation: In medical diagnostics, such as assessing coronary artery disease from perfusion data or prostate cancer from dynamic biomarkers, accurate model identification from limited, noisy patient data is critical. Traditional system identification often uses arbitrary or standard test signals, which may be suboptimal for complex, patient-specific physiological models with memory and nonlinear dynamics. Generating optimal input signals that maximize information gain can significantly improve diagnostic reliability and reduce examination time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a reinforcement learning agent, guided by a dual objective of model information gain and physiological safety constraints, can generate input signals that lead to more precise and robust identification of patient-specific physiological models compared to classical optimal input design methods.
proposals.method: We will frame the input design as a sequential decision problem. A deep reinforcement learning agent (e.g., a DDPG or PPO algorithm) will interact with a known, patient-specific simulator (the 'model') to design the next input. The reward function will combine the trace of the Fisher Information Matrix (for D-optimality) with constraints on input amplitude and rate of change. To handle the temporal dependencies of physiological systems, we will integrate a reconfigurable multi-access pattern vector memory, inspired by recent work on real-time feature extraction, to allow the agent to efficiently store and recall sequences of state-estimate covariances.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, adaptive framework for optimal input design tailored to biomedical system identification. It will provide concrete algorithms for generating safer, more informative diagnostic test protocols. The integration of advanced memory architectures with RL for a dual control problem represents a methodological advance for control theory applications in medicine.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying modern RL with specialized memory systems to the classical 'dual problem' of control, specifically for physiological models where safety and information are co-primary. This moves beyond linear-quadratic-Gaussian assumptions and static experiment design prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Robust Relay Autotuning and Control under Plant Parameter Uncertainty via Dual Extended Kalman Filtering
proposals.motivation: Classical relay autotuners, pioneered by Åström and Hägglund, assume a known static process model after identification to compute PID parameters. In real-world applications like biomedical systems (e.g., drug delivery, physiological control), model parameters can drift or be uncertain due to patient-specific variability (e.g., changing arterial compliance, tumor response). This uncertainty compromises the robustness and performance of controllers derived from standard autotuning procedures.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a dual estimation framework that concurrently identifies a time-varying process model and its parameter uncertainty bounds, a relay autotuner can generate robust PID controller parameters that guarantee stability and performance despite ongoing parameter drift.
proposals.method: We will extend the Next-Generation Relay Autotuner architecture with a Dual Extended Kalman Filter (DEKF). One filter will estimate the system state (e.g., critical output variables), while a second, parallel filter will continuously estimate the parameters of a low-order transfer function model (e.g., FOPDT). The estimated parameter covariance matrix will inform a subsequent robust control synthesis step, employing a loop-shaping design or a convex optimization (e.g., using the Youla parameterization) that explicitly accounts for the quantified uncertainty. The method will be validated in a high-fidelity simulation of a prostate cancer hyperthermia treatment system, where perfusion (modeled as a time-varying gain and time constant) is uncertain.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal methodology for robust autotuning, bridging the gap between classical industrial tuning and advanced robust control theory. It will deliver an implementable algorithm that enhances the reliability of autonomous control in safety-critical applications with inherent patient variability, directly contributing to the field of adaptive and robust biomedical control systems.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of DEKF-based real-time parameter and uncertainty estimation within the relay autotuning framework moves beyond point-estimate adaptation, enabling robust controller synthesis. This addresses a core limitation in traditional autotuners, which lack a formal mechanism to handle the uncertainty they themselves identify.

proposals.title: Friction Model Parameter Estimation via Recursive PEM and Multi-Experiment Relay-Based Autotuning
proposals.motivation: Accurate friction modeling is critical for high-precision motion control in robotics and mechatronics, yet identifying complex, nonlinear friction parameters (e.g., Stribeck, viscous, Coulomb) from operational data remains challenging. Pre-2026, advanced system identification techniques from signal processing and Åström's autotuning legacy offer a powerful but underexplored toolkit for this problem, promising more robust and automated parameter estimation than traditional manual curve-fitting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a tailored combination of Prediction Error Methods (PEM) and next-generation relay autotuners can robustly and automatically estimate the parameters of complex, nonlinear friction models from closed-loop experimental data, outperforming standard least-squares and manual methods in accuracy and repeatability.
proposals.method: We will adapt and implement a recursive Prediction Error Method (PEM) framework, using a nonlinear friction model (e.g., LuGre) within the predictor. To enrich excitation for persistent identification, we will integrate a reconfigurable multi-experiment relay autotuner, inspired by Åström's recent work, to generate informative input signals. The algorithm will be validated on a high-fidelity simulated servo system and a real-time experimental setup with a reconfigurable vector memory architecture for efficient data handling during ORB-like feature extraction from the identification signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, automated procedure for high-fidelity friction identification, directly applicable to precision motion systems. It will extend the application domain of relay autotuning and PEM to nonlinear static parameter estimation, providing a concrete software tool and benchmark results for the control community.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific fusion of recursive PEM for nonlinear static models with modern, multi-experiment relay autotuning to solve the friction identification problem, an approach distinct from generic machine learning or offline optimization methods prevalent pre-2026.

ideas.title: Adapt Kalman Filtering from Navigation for Time-Varying System Identification
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption of time-invariant parameters by drawing an analogy from aerospace navigation and state estimation. The analogous problem is tracking a vehicle's state (position, velocity) using noisy sensor data when the dynamics might change. This is solved using Kalman filtering, a recursive algorithm that maintains estimates of both the state and the uncertainty, optimally blending model predictions with new measurements. Adaptive versions can also estimate unknown parameters. Adapted to system identification, we formulate the time-varying parameters of a linear system as part of an augmented state vector that follows a presumed stochastic dynamic model (like a random walk). An extended or adaptive Kalman filter is then employed to perform online, recursive identification. This provides a stream of optimal parameter estimates and their confidence bounds, inherently designed for time-variation, as opposed to batch methods that assume fixed parameters.

ideas.title: Invert known system parameters assumption
ideas.description: Create adaptive methods for simultaneous parameter identification and state estimation or control.

ideas.title: Improve computational efficiency of filter implementations
ideas.description: Create numerical methods to reduce the computational burden of Kalman filter designs for high-dimensional systems.

ideas.title: Relax the requirement for known system order
ideas.description: Develop adaptive controllers for systems with unknown or varying dimensionality.

ideas.title: Adaptive Experiment Design for Parameter Identification with Unknown Control Direction
ideas.description: This research direction develops a unified adaptive control and experiment design strategy for systems with completely unknown control direction. The core idea is to co-design the stabilizing adaptive controller and the probing signals for parameter estimation, such that the system is persistently excited for identification while simultaneously learning the correct control direction online. This eliminates the need for any prior knowledge of the sign of the control gain, a major limitation in classical adaptive system identification. The resulting scheme guarantees stability and convergence of parameter estimates from a 'cold start' with zero prior structural knowledge, enabling reliable black-box identification for autonomous tuning and digital twin creation in uncertain environments.

ideas.title: Real-Time, Controller-Codesigned Experimentation for Lightweight Parameter Learning
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework for lightweight, real-time parameter identification in embedded control systems. It co-designs the stabilizing controller and the parameter estimation experiment, allowing the controller to actively generate minimal, informative control signals that maximize learning while strictly respecting real-time computational, memory, and safety constraints. The result is an adaptive embedded system that can perform precise online model refinement and fault detection under severe resource limitations, a capability impossible if experiment design and runtime identification are treated as separate, heavyweight processes.

ideas.title: Scale analysis to multi-rate sampled systems
ideas.description: Extend the package to analyze systems with multiple different sampling rates.

ideas.title: Scale down for embedded and real-time use
ideas.description: Create lightweight identification methods for resource-constrained real-time applications.

ideas.title: Lightweight System Identification via Compressed Sensing and Edge-Pruned Models
ideas.description: This research expands the seed idea 'Scale down for embedded and real-time use' by drawing an analogy from medical imaging. In MRI, Compressed Sensing solves the problem of long scan times (a resource constraint) by acquiring undersampled data and exploiting signal sparsity for reconstruction. We adapt this solution to lightweight system identification. The core idea is to create identification methods that use compressed sensing principles: acquiring sparse, informative temporal samples from a target system and employing efficient, edge-optimized reconstruction algorithms to fit a parsimonious model (e.g., a pruned network or low-order linear model). This approach minimizes data collection and computational overhead, making it feasible for resource-constrained, real-time embedded applications where traditional identification is too heavy.

ideas.title: Observer Design via Compressed Sensing for Performance Guarantees with Partial Outputs
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that full state measurement is necessary for performance guarantees in control or estimation. Drawing an analogy from medical imaging, where compressed sensing reconstructs images from severely undersampled and noisy frequency data, we adapt this core principle. The adapted research idea is to design an observer for dynamical systems that achieves performance guarantees using only limited or noisy output signals. This is done by formulating state reconstruction as a compressed sensing problem, exploiting sparsity or compressibility of the state trajectory in a known basis (e.g., Fourier, wavelet, or a learned dictionary). The solution involves solving a regularized optimization that enforces consistency with the sparse dynamics model and the few available measurements. Theoretical guarantees on estimation error and, consequently, closed-loop performance can then be provided based on the compressibility of the state and the properties of the system's observability matrix, analogous to the RIP in classical compressed sensing.

ideas.title: Use system identification from time series analysis
ideas.description: Incorporate time-series model identification techniques to learn system dynamics for the filter.

ideas.title: Improve errors-in-variables identification techniques
ideas.description: Advance methods for system identification when both inputs and outputs are observed with noise.

ideas.title: Address nonlinear or time-varying processes
ideas.description: Extend the autotuning framework to handle nonlinear dynamics or parameters that drift.
ideas.sourceTitle: Automatic tuning of simple regulators with specifications on phase and amplitude margins

ideas.title: Extend nonlinear estimation to more general system classes
ideas.description: Develop filters for nonlinear systems beyond the specific cases covered, like those with non-additive noise or discontinuous dynamics.

ideas.title: Asymptotic Theory for Adaptive Control with Order-Agnostic System Identification
ideas.description: This research develops a unified asymptotic theory for adaptive control systems where the plant order is unknown. It extends classical stochastic approximation and ODE methods to analyze controllers coupled with modern identification algorithms (e.g., sparse or regularization-based estimators, variational methods) that can infer system dimensionality from data. The core novelty is providing convergence guarantees and performance bounds for the joint process of parameter and order estimation, leading to provably stable adaptive control without the classical assumption of a known, fixed system order. This enables formally justified controllers for complex systems with latent or evolving structures.

ideas.title: Errors-in-Variables System Identification with Unknown Control Direction and Gain Magnitude
ideas.description: This research direction develops novel system identification and adaptive control algorithms for scenarios where both the applied control signal and the measured output are corrupted by significant, potentially correlated, noise (errors-in-variables), and the sign and scale of the high-frequency gain are unknown. The core synergy lies in co-designing the parameter estimator and the adaptive controller: the identification must be robust to input noise while simultaneously providing credible confidence intervals or parameter sets to guide a Nussbaum-gain or similar direction-seeking control law. This enables stable, data-driven control of poorly understood physical systems where actuator signals are inferred indirectly from noisy sensors, moving beyond methods that assume either perfect input knowledge or a known control direction.

ideas.title: Scale down to microsecond embedded systems
ideas.description: Implement the autotuning algorithm on low-power microcontrollers for small-scale applications.
ideas.sourceTitle: Automatic tuning of simple regulators with specifications on phase and amplitude margins

ideas.title: Invert the assumption of known model parameters
ideas.description: Solve the problem under parameter uncertainty, requiring filtering or robust control methods.
ideas.sourceTitle: Optimal control of Markov processes with incomplete state information

ideas.title: Consider computational limits for large-scale systems
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption of unlimited computational power for analyzing high-order sampled systems.

ideas.title: Adaptive Seismic Imaging for Controller-Guided Parameter Probing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the inverse problem in seismic exploration, where optimal source signals are designed to probe subsurface structures, to the domain of dynamical systems. The analogous field is Geophysics. There, the solution involves designing seismic wave inputs (e.g., chirps) based on prior models to maximize information gain about specific underground parameters during full-waveform inversion. Adapted to the original research context, the stabilizing controller is used not just for regulation, but as a tool to design optimal probing experiments. By synthesizing control inputs or reference trajectories that optimally excite the system's modes related to uncertain parameters, the controller actively shapes the experiment to produce output data with maximum Fisher information for those parameters. This creates a dual-use for the controller: stabilization and intelligent experiment design for scaling precise parameter estimation.

ideas.title: Improved auto-tuning for oscillatory systems
ideas.description: Enhance auto-tuning algorithms specifically for systems with significant oscillatory dynamics.
ideas.sourceTitle: PID Controllers: Theory, Design, and Tuning

ideas.title: Neural Network-Based Concurrent Identification and Stabilization for Unstable Nonlinear Systems
ideas.description: This research direction develops a unified algorithm that merges neural network-based adaptive backstepping control with system identification for unstable nonlinear systems. The core idea is to use the neural networks within the backstepping procedure not only to approximate unknown nonlinearities and stabilize the system but also to serve as a parametric model for an online identifier. The stabilizing controller ensures bounded signals, creating a safe operational envelope from which informative time-series data can be extracted for concurrent model learning. This closed-loop identification approach enables the refinement of the neural model's knowledge of the unstable dynamics during operation, leading to progressively improved control performance and model accuracy.

ideas.title: Use system identification for auto-tuning
ideas.description: Incorporate online system identification algorithms to continuously update process models for auto-tuning.
ideas.sourceTitle: PID Controllers: Theory, Design, and Tuning

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysical Blind Deconvolution for Errors-in-Variables System Identification
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to advance errors-in-variables (EIV) identification techniques by drawing an analogy from geophysical blind deconvolution. In seismic processing, a recorded seismogram results from the convolution of an unknown source wavelet with an unknown Earth reflectivity series, a problem solved using statistical priors and joint estimation. Adapting this solution, the research will develop novel system identification methods for dynamic systems where both inputs and outputs are observed with noise. The core idea is to treat the noise-free input as a latent variable, incorporate application-specific physical or statistical priors on this input and the system parameters, and formulate a joint estimation problem—potentially using sparse Bayesian learning or variational inference—to simultaneously identify the system model and denoise the input-output data, thereby improving the accuracy and robustness of EIV identification.

ideas.title: Allow for completely unknown control direction
ideas.description: Design adaptive schemes that do not require prior knowledge of the control gain sign.

ideas.title: Dual problem: data generation from known models
ideas.description: Generate optimal input signals to maximally inform the identification process.

ideas.title: Examine computation of OPENAT transfer functions
ideas.description: Investigate and improve the algorithms for computing OPENAT transfer functions in sampled-data analysis.

ideas.title: Robust Adaptive Identification with Graceful Degradation for Safety-Critical Embedded Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a robust, adaptive identification system for resource-constrained real-time applications that guarantees functional safety under uncertainty. Instead of a single static lightweight model, the system employs a hierarchical framework with a primary fast identifier, a confidence estimator, and an on-demand robust verifier. When the confidence estimator detects ambiguous, noisy, or out-of-distribution inputs, the system dynamically switches to a safer operational mode, such as invoking the verifier or executing a predefined fail-safe policy. This approach ensures that the identification system maintains core reliability and safety guarantees even when the primary lightweight method is insufficient, making it suitable for demanding embedded applications like autonomous robotics and industrial control.

ideas.title: Consider systems with significant time delays
ideas.description: Adapt the tuning method for processes with large or variable dead times.
ideas.sourceTitle: Automatic tuning of simple regulators with specifications on phase and amplitude margins

ideas.title: Scale up to distributed sensor networks
ideas.description: Develop decentralized Kalman filtering for large-scale systems with multiple, communicating sensors.

ideas.title: Validate method with physical experiments
ideas.description: Test the proposed autotuning technique on real hardware, not just in simulation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Automatic tuning of simple regulators with specifications on phase and amplitude margins

ideas.title: Apply system identification techniques for model learning
ideas.description: Integrate online system identification methods to improve model reference adaptation.

ideas.title: Robust Identification of Systems with Partial and Noisy Input-Output Trajectories
ideas.description: This research direction develops novel system identification algorithms for the joint problem of incomplete data sequences and errors-in-variables conditions. It aims to create estimators that can reconstruct missing segments of input and/or output trajectories while simultaneously deconvolving the measurement noise corrupting the observed portions, without requiring complete, noise-free anchor points. The core synergy lies in formulating a single likelihood or cost function that penalizes model mismatch, penalizes noise energy, and incorporates structured priors or constraints on the plausible evolution of the missing data, enabling statistically consistent identification where traditional methods would fail or be severely biased.

ideas.title: Scale down to micro-systems with limited computation
ideas.description: Design extremely lightweight filter variants for embedded systems with severe memory and processing constraints.

ideas.title: Dual problem: Design for performance optimization, not just stability
ideas.description: Formulate and solve an optimal control problem using the backstepping framework.

ideas.title: Data-Driven Adaptive Recursive Passivity: Learning-Based Structure Selection for Interconnection-Based Control Synthesis
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes statistical learning for model selection with recursive passivity-based control. The core idea is to use data-driven techniques—specifically cross-validation and regularization—to automatically determine the optimal structure and complexity of a recursively constructed passivity-based control law, such as in adaptive backstepping or interconnected system design. Instead of pre-defining the number of recursive steps or the interconnection topology based on a nominal model, the framework learns the most parsimonious, stabilizing structure directly from operational data. This results in a novel adaptive control synthesis method that guarantees stability through passivity while being data-efficient and robust to model order uncertainty, bridging the gap between purely model-based recursive design and black-box learning.

ideas.title: Remove the need for full state measurement
ideas.description: Achieve performance guarantees using only limited or noisy output signals.

ideas.title: Relax need for detailed robot model knowledge
ideas.description: Develop adaptive controllers that require minimal or no prior model information.

ideas.title: Stability-Robust Dynamic Order Selection via Regime-Aware Resampling for MIMO Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a new model order and structure selection framework for MIMO systems that replaces asymptotic information criteria with a direct, finite-sample generalization error estimate. The method employs a regime-aware block bootstrap procedure to generate resampled datasets that preserve the temporal correlation structure and distinct operational phases of the dynamic system. The final selection criterion penalizes both empirical fit and a quantified measure of model instability, calculated from the parameter variance across these resampled trajectories, ensuring the chosen model generalizes well beyond the single observed dataset. This approach is specifically designed for the challenges of short, noisy, and closed-loop data where traditional criteria fail.

ideas.title: Transfer system identification techniques
ideas.description: Use advanced system identification methods from signal processing to accurately estimate complex friction model parameters.

ideas.title: Robust Modular Adaptive Control with Concurrent Learning for Systems with Fully Unknown Actuation Dynamics
ideas.description: This approach develops a modular adaptive controller that guarantees stability and performance for systems with completely unknown control direction and gain magnitude, without assuming persistent excitation. It combines a robust monitoring function to eliminate parameter drift with an event-triggered switching mechanism that efficiently explores the control direction. A key innovation is the use of a concurrent learning estimator, which analyzes stored input-output data to quickly identify the actuation sign and gain, enabling a rapid transition from exploration to high-performance model-based adaptation.

ideas.title: Formulate the inverse optimal control problem
ideas.description: Given an observed control law, infer the cost function the system is implicitly optimizing.

ideas.title: Use neural networks for function approximation in backstepping
ideas.description: Employ neural networks to approximate unknown nonlinear functions within the recursive design.

ideas.title: Analyze systems with non-uniform sampling
ideas.description: Relax the assumption of uniform sampling periods in the analysis of sampled-data systems.

ideas.title: Transfer signal processing spectral methods
ideas.description: Employ frequency-domain and spectral estimation techniques for linear system identification.

ideas.title: Provable Safety with Unobservable Dynamics via Adaptive Uncertainty Quantification and Filtering
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond seeking performance guarantees from limited outputs alone and instead provides certifiable safety. It employs an adaptive estimation framework that produces a real-time, high-probability bound on the full system state's deviation, explicitly accounting for noise, model uncertainty, and unobservable subspaces. This uncertainty bound is then used by a runtime safety assurance module, which acts as a filter on any primary control signal. The system thus delivers verifiable safety guarantees despite limited sensing, while maximizing performance by intervening only when the quantified risk of leaving the safe set exceeds a strict threshold.

ideas.title: Dual-Optimization for Informative Stabilization: Co-Designing Control and Experimentation for Parameter Learning
ideas.description: This approach reframes the problem as a dual-objective optimal control challenge, simultaneously ensuring system stability and maximizing information gain for parameter identification. The core innovation is a real-time, constrained optimization that computes control inputs which are guaranteed to keep the system within a safe, stable region while actively probing its dynamics in the most informative directions. This is achieved by integrating stability constraints, such as barrier functions or Lyapunov-based conditions, directly into an information-maximizing objective. The result is an adaptive, closed-loop experimentation policy that safely learns precise system parameters without sacrificing operational safety or performance.

ideas.title: Adapting Compressed Sensing for Output-Feedback in Complex Nonlinear Systems
ideas.description: Extend the output-feedback results to more complex nonlinear system structures by drawing an analogy from compressed sensing in signal processing. The analogous solution involves reconstructing a high-dimensional sparse signal from few measurements via l1-norm optimization. Adapted to control, the unmeasured state of a nonlinear system is treated as a structured signal. The research idea is to design systematic output-feedback laws that actively reconstruct the state in real-time by solving an optimization problem constrained by the known structural properties of the system's nonlinearities (e.g., bounded Jacobians, sector conditions, or sparse interconnections), thereby enabling stabilization and performance for broader, more complex classes than those addressable by standard observer-based methods.

ideas.title: System Identification via Expectation-Maximization and Imputation, Inspired by Paleoclimatology
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that system identification requires complete input-output data sequences. Drawing an analogy from paleoclimatology, where scientists reconstruct continuous climate histories from sparse, indirect proxy records, we propose adapting data assimilation and Expectation-Maximization techniques. The analogous solution uses a physical or statistical model in conjunction with incomplete observations to iteratively impute missing data and refine the model. Adapted to system identification, this involves developing an algorithm that treats the unknown system dynamics as the target 'physics'. The algorithm iterates between an E-step, which probabilistically infers missing inputs, outputs, or internal states given the current system model and the observed data fragments, and an M-step, which performs standard system identification on the now-complete (though partially imputed) dataset. This approach allows for the identification of dynamical models from partially observed, gappy, or irregularly sampled time-series data, effectively relaxing the requirement for complete data records.

ideas.title: Refine information criteria for dynamic specification
ideas.description: Develop more robust information criteria for selecting model order and structure in MIMO systems.

ideas.title: Solve the inverse problem of controller design
ideas.description: Use identified system models to directly synthesize optimal controllers.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Design tasks for controller testing
ideas.description: Systematically design robotic tasks that best reveal adaptive controller limitations.

ideas.description: Apply the autotuning technique to multivariable systems with higher dimensions or more complex interactions.

ideas.title: Develop numerical algorithms for extension problems
ideas.description: Create computational methods to construct positive definite extensions from sampled data.
ideas.sourceTitle: Comparison of Riemann and Lebesgue sampling for first order stochastic systems

ideas.title: A Unifying Framework for Asymptotic Analysis Based on Algorithmic Stability Properties
ideas.description: This research establishes a rigorous, property-based framework to extend classical asymptotic theory to modern, computationally-driven estimators. Instead of treating each algorithm in isolation, it defines a minimal set of stability and convergence properties (e.g., local uniform convergence of the algorithmic objective function) that guarantee standard asymptotic results. The core work involves verifying these properties for broad classes of iterative, simulation-based, and regularized identification procedures, thereby providing a unified 'certificate' of asymptotic validity. This enables the systematic derivation of inference methods, such as robust standard errors and bootstrap procedures, that are automatically valid for any estimator proven to fit within the framework.

ideas.title: Apply numerical optimization algorithms
ideas.description: Use advanced iterative optimization methods to solve the maximum likelihood estimation problem.

ideas.title: Adaptive Control via Online Model Selection Inspired by Phylogenetics
ideas.description: This idea expands the challenge of relaxing the known system order requirement by drawing an analogy from computational phylogenetics, where the true evolutionary model (including its complexity) is unknown. The analogous solution uses techniques like Bayesian Model Averaging to dynamically explore a space of models with varying dimensionality. Adapted to control theory, this leads to the development of an adaptive controller that treats system order as a latent variable. The controller employs an online probabilistic inference framework (e.g., a particle filter with model jumping or a variational Bayes approach) to jointly estimate the state, parameters, and the most plausible system order from streaming data. The control law is then computed based on a weighted combination of controllers designed for these candidate models, resulting in a resilient adaptive controller that can handle unknown or time-varying system dimensionality without prior commitment to a fixed model structure.

ideas.title: Extend adaptive methods to broader robot classes
ideas.description: Apply and test reviewed adaptive methods on more complex robotic system types.

ideas.title: Compare performance with other autotuning methods
ideas.description: Benchmark the novel fractional order method against existing integer-order autotuners.
ideas.sourceTitle: Automatic tuning of simple regulators with specifications on phase and amplitude margins

ideas.title: Safe Experimental Identification of Unstable Systems via Informative Stabilization
ideas.description: This research develops a practical framework for identifying unstable linear systems by designing experiments that are both safe and informative. It integrates a robust, minimally invasive stabilizing controller during data collection to prevent signal blow-up and equipment damage. The methodology then employs tailored closed-loop identification techniques to accurately recover the open-loop unstable dynamics from the stabilized experimental data. This approach transforms the problem from a theoretically ill-posed one into a tractable experimental procedure with guaranteed safety and excitation properties.

ideas.title: PID structure is fixed and optimal
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that the standard three-term PID structure is the most efficient form.

ideas.title: Robust Adaptive Sliding Mode Control for High-Dimensional MIMO Systems with Online Sparse Identification
ideas.description: This research develops a novel integrated architecture for the robust control of very high-dimensional Multi-Input Multi-Output (MIMO) systems subject to significant disturbances and model uncertainties. The core innovation is the tight coupling of a scalable, sparse system identification algorithm—which continuously estimates a parsimonious model of the dominant system dynamics in real-time—with an adaptive sliding mode control (SMC) law synthesized via backstepping. The identifier actively informs the choice of the sliding surface and the adaptive gains, allowing the controller to remain robust against mismatched disturbances and time-varying parameters while maintaining computational feasibility. This synergy enables guaranteed stability and performance for large-scale systems where full-model-based SMC design is prohibitive and where traditional identification methods are decoupled from the robust control synthesis.

ideas.title: Compressed Sensing for High-Dimensional MIMO System Identification
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution of compressed sensing from MRI and signal processing to the problem of identifying very high-dimensional MIMO systems. In MRI, compressed sensing allows for the reconstruction of high-quality images from far fewer measurements than traditionally required by exploiting signal sparsity. Analogously, we propose to develop computationally efficient system identification algorithms that exploit the inherent low-rank, sparse, or compressible structure of the high-dimensional MIMO channel matrix (e.g., in a spatial beam or virtual channel domain). By designing tailored probe signals and recovery algorithms based on convex optimization or greedy methods, the system can be identified from a drastically reduced set of training observations, overcoming the curse of dimensionality associated with exhaustive input-output testing.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Deconvolution for System Identification via Multitaper Spectral Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the seismic deconvolution problem from geophysics for linear system identification. In seismic processing, multitaper spectral estimation is used to stabilize the deconvolution needed to recover the Earth's impulse response from noisy recordings. The adapted research idea employs this same approach: using multitaper methods to produce reliable spectral estimates of input and output signals, enabling a robust frequency-domain calculation of the system's transfer function via spectral division. This method is particularly suited for identifying systems where data is limited and contaminated by noise, providing a statistically stable alternative to traditional periodogram-based spectral analysis or time-domain least-squares fitting.

ideas.title: Relax requirement for complete input-output data
ideas.description: Handle system identification with partially observed or missing data sequences.

ideas.description: Train models to learn the mapping from partial positive definite data to full extensions.

ideas.title: Micro-scale PID for nano-manipulation and MEMS
ideas.description: Design and implement PID control for systems with micro-second dynamics and nanometer precision.

ideas.title: Decentralized PID for large-scale networked systems
ideas.description: Design PID controllers for systems where process variables are distributed and communication is limited.

ideas.title: Virtual Passivity and Dynamic Scaling for Recursive Stabilization Beyond Strict-Feedback Form
ideas.description: This research develops a recursive control design methodology that generalizes passivity-based backstepping to systems not in strict-feedback form. By combining the concepts of virtual control passivation, dynamic scaling factors, and immersion and invariance, the approach constructs a sequence of target manifolds that enforce passivity properties on transformed error dynamics. The core innovation is a scaling-based adaptation law that recursively ensures the attractivity of these manifolds, systematically building a Lyapunov function. This results in a robust, modular design procedure applicable to a broader class of nonlinear systems, including those with non-triangular structures and uncertain parameters, while retaining the stability and interconnection insights of passivity theory.

ideas.title: Econometric Model Diagnostics as a Filter for Robust Dynamic System Identification
ideas.description: This research develops a robust, two-stage system identification framework that integrates econometric rigor with control-oriented modeling. In the first stage, candidate models from engineering methods (e.g., subspace identification, prediction error minimization) are subjected to a battery of econometric specification tests for misspecification, such as tests for parameter constancy, uncorrelated innovations, and neglected nonlinearity. Models failing these diagnostics are rejected. In the second stage, the surviving models undergo final evaluation and selection based on their closed-loop predictive performance and control-relevant metrics. This ensures the final model is not only a good statistical representation of the data but also reliable for prediction and controller design.

ideas.title: Use statistical learning for model order selection
ideas.description: Apply cross-validation and regularization from statistics to select system structure.

ideas.title: Robust Hybrid Optimization with Adaptive Algorithm Switching for Maximum Likelihood Estimation
ideas.description: This system addresses the fragility of applying a single advanced optimization algorithm to complex MLE problems by implementing a two-stage, diagnostically-driven hybrid framework. First, a global exploration phase uses model-based, derivative-free optimization to intelligently sample the parameter space and identify regions with high likelihood potential, mitigating the risk of converging to poor local optima. Second, an adaptive local optimization phase employs real-time monitoring of convergence diagnostics, gradient quality, and Hessian approximations to automatically switch between specialized numerical algorithms (e.g., switching from BFGS to a trust-region method if ill-conditioning is detected). This approach ensures robust and efficient convergence across a wide range of problematic likelihood surfaces where any single algorithm would fail.

ideas.title: Scale up to large, distributed multi-robot systems
ideas.description: Extend adaptive control to coordinate large fleets of interacting robots.

ideas.title: Transfer function approximation from circuit theory
ideas.description: Use model reduction techniques to simplify high-order systems for computationally feasible filter design.

ideas.title: Dependency-Aware Model Order Selection via Structured Resampling and Regularization
ideas.description: This approach addresses the core failure of standard statistical learning methods when selecting model order for systems with dependent data. It combines tailored resampling schemes that respect the inherent data structure (e.g., temporal blocking, spatial folds) with regularization techniques designed for dependent errors or graphical models. The system first infers or incorporates prior knowledge of the dependency type, then applies a corresponding validation framework that prevents leakage, yielding robust and consistent structure selection for time series, spatial processes, and network data.

ideas.title: Compositional Nonlinear System Identification via Structural Meta-Architectures
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for nonlinear system identification that directly combats the curse of dimensionality inherent in a generic nonlinear search. Instead of relaxing the linearity assumption entirely, it uses the structural principles from linear theory (e.g., series/parallel connections, feedback loops, and subsystem decomposition) as a blueprint for constructing nonlinear models. The method defines a meta-architecture where these structural templates are populated with parameterized, tractable nonlinear components, constraining the model space to physically plausible and identifiable structures. This results in identification algorithms that are both data-efficient and scalable, providing a principled bridge from linear structural insights to practical nonlinear modeling.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Active Learning for Data-Efficient High-Dimensional MIMO Identification
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for identifying very high-dimensional MIMO systems under severe data constraints. It combines two key innovations: a model architecture that hardcodes known physical laws and domain-specific constraints as invariances or regularization terms, reducing the hypothesis space, and an active learning loop that optimally designs excitation signals to minimize parameter uncertainty. The approach guarantees that identified models are physically plausible and generalizable, even from limited experiments. This results in robust, computationally tractable identification for complex systems like large-scale fluid flows, power grids, or massive antenna arrays where exhaustive data collection is impossible.

ideas.title: Relax the no-process-model assumption
ideas.description: Incorporate partial or grey-box model knowledge to improve tuning accuracy or speed.

ideas.description: Use the extension framework to solve concrete spectral analysis problems in signal processing.
